shortDefinition: Abandonment is when a contractor stops work on a job and walks away without a legal excuse, leaving it unfinished. In Arizona it is a specific ground for disciplining a contractor's license, and it lets an affected homeowner file a complaint with the Registrar of Contractors.
definitionHtml:
Abandonment is the construction word for a contractor quitting and walking away. Arizona law treats it as a serious offense, not just a private contract dispute. A.R.S. 32-1154 lists the grounds the Registrar of Contractors (ROC) can use to suspend or revoke a license, and the very first one is "abandonment of a contract or refusal to perform after submitting a bid on work without legal excuse for the abandonment or refusal."

The key phrase is without legal excuse. A contractor who stops work because you stopped paying, or because the site is unsafe, may have a legal reason to halt. A contractor who simply stops showing up, cashes a draw, and goes silent does not. That second situation is abandonment, and it gives you a clear basis to file an ROC complaint against the builder's license.

Abandonment usually shows itself the same way: communication dries up, the crew disappears for weeks, and the work sits at a partial stage while your money is already spent. Because Arizona pays contractors on a draw schedule tied to completed phases, an abandoned job often leaves you with a half-built house and a draw or two already gone.

For example, a Casa Grande homeowner pays the framing draw, then the builder stops answering calls and never returns to start the next phase. With no legal excuse for the stoppage, the owner can file an ROC complaint citing A.R.S. 32-1154 abandonment, and, if the builder was licensed, may pursue a Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund claim for the cost to finish.

If you suspect abandonment, document everything: the contract, the payment record, the dates the crew last worked, and your attempts to reach the builder. That record is what the ROC investigates, and it is also what protects you if the dispute moves to a recovery-fund claim or court.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 32-1154 (Grounds for suspension or revocation of license)
  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors, Recovery Fund
metaDescription: Abandonment is when a contractor quits a job without legal excuse. Here is how Arizona defines it, the ROC discipline it triggers, and what owners can do.

shortDefinition: An allowance is a set dollar amount in a building contract for an item you have not chosen yet, such as flooring, cabinets, or appliances. The builder budgets the allowance into the price; if your final selection costs more, you pay the difference, and if it costs less, you get credited.
definitionHtml:
When you sign a construction contract, you have not picked every finish yet, things like tile, light fixtures, countertops, or appliances. So the builder writes in a dollar amount for each undecided category and includes it in the total price. That budgeted figure is an allowance, a placeholder price that lets the contract be signed before every selection is final.

The key rule is reconciliation. If your actual choice costs more than the allowance, you pay the overage. If it costs less, you receive a credit. For example, a contract might carry a $12,000 flooring allowance. If you later choose tile and carpet that come to $15,000 installed, you owe the $3,000 difference; if you spend $10,000, you save $2,000. Allowances cover the product and sometimes the labor to install it, so it matters whether the number is "material only" or "installed," and you confirm that before signing.

Allowances keep a project moving without forcing every decision up front, but a low allowance can hide the real cost of a home. A builder can quote an attractive total by setting thin allowances, then the price climbs as you make realistic selections. Reading the allowance schedule line by line tells you whether the budget is honest.

An allowance differs from a fixed-price line, where the item and its cost are locked in, and from a change order, which alters the scope of work after the contract is set. Allowances cover known categories with unknown selections; change orders cover new or different work. We set allowances to reflect the finishes most clients actually pick, so the contract price stays close to the final cost, including upgrades like a casita or premium fixtures.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors
  • U.S. Federal Trade Commission, How To Avoid a Home Improvement Scam
metaDescription: An allowance is a budgeted dollar amount in a building contract for items not yet chosen. Here is how allowances work, and how cost overages are handled.

shortDefinition: The Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) is the state agency that licenses, regulates, and disciplines construction contractors. It sets license classes, takes consumer complaints about poor work, and runs the Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund that can reimburse homeowners up to $30,000 when a licensed builder fails.
definitionHtml:
Under A.R.S. 32-1101, anyone who builds, alters, or repairs a structure for pay is a "contractor" and must hold a license from the state agency that licenses and polices the people who build in Arizona: the Arizona Registrar of Contractors, almost always shortened to ROC (or AZ ROC). The agency sets the license classes, checks that applicants meet experience and exam requirements, and can suspend or revoke a license for cause.

The ROC does three jobs that matter to a homeowner. First, licensing: it issues and renews contractor licenses and publishes a searchable license lookup so you can confirm a builder is current before you sign. Second, complaints: if a licensed contractor does poor work or walks off the job, you can file a written complaint and the ROC investigates, inspects, and can order the contractor to fix the problem or face discipline. Third, the Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund: a consumer-protection fund every residential contractor pays into.

The Recovery Fund is the part most owners do not know about. If a licensed residential contractor does shoddy or unfinished work and you cannot collect from them directly, the fund can reimburse you up to $30,000 per residence, with a cap of $200,000 per contractor license. You must have hired a licensed contractor (the fund does not cover unlicensed work) and follow the ROC's claim steps.

For example, a Cave Creek homeowner hires a licensed builder for a custom home. The builder takes the draws but leaves the framing defective and stops returning calls. The owner files an ROC complaint, the ROC investigates and rules against the builder, and the owner files a Recovery Fund claim for the cost to repair, up to the $30,000 cap.

The ROC is also why hiring a licensed contractor matters so much in Arizona. An unlicensed contractor is outside this whole system: no recovery fund, no disciplinary recourse, and under state law they often cannot even sue you to collect. When you check a builder, you are really checking their standing with the ROC.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors, About AZ ROC
  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors, Recovery Fund
  • A.R.S. 32-1101 (Definitions; contractor; registrar)
metaDescription: The Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) licenses builders, takes complaints, and runs the recovery fund. Here is what the ROC does for Arizona homeowners.

shortDefinition: An as-completed appraisal estimates what a home will be worth once it is finished, based on the plans and specifications, not its value today as a vacant lot or half-built shell. Construction lenders use this future value to set how much they will lend on a build.
definitionHtml:
There is no finished house to appraise when a construction loan is made, often just dirt or a partial structure, so the appraiser has to work from the plans, specifications, and finishes instead. The result is an as-completed appraisal, a value that estimates what the finished home will be worth on the open market rather than what exists today. That future number is the figure a construction lender cares about.

Lenders use the as-completed value to set how much they will lend. Your loan amount is capped by a loan-to-value (LTV) ratio against that future value, and on a construction-to-permanent loan it also factors against total cost. So if the as-completed appraisal comes in at $900,000, the lender lends a percentage of that, which in turn tells you how much cash or land equity you need to bring to close the gap. A low appraisal can shrink the loan and force you to cover more out of pocket.

The appraisal is tied to your exact plans, which is why selections matter. Fannie Mae's guidance for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans uses the "as completed" appraised value, and requires a completion report at the end to confirm the home was built to those plans. If the finished home does not match, or the appraiser later finds the value declined, the lender can require a new appraisal and requalify the borrower.

For example, a Cave Creek borrower submits final plans and a finish schedule, and the appraiser, using comparable finished homes nearby, sets an as-completed value of $950,000. The lender sizes the construction loan from that number, not from the bare lot's current value.

Because the whole loan rests on it, give the appraiser complete, accurate plans and a real finish schedule. An honest, well-supported as-completed appraisal is what makes the construction loan, the interest reserve, and the draw schedule all add up.

Sources

  • U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, What is a construction loan?
  • Fannie Mae Selling Guide, Construction-to-Permanent Financing (single-closing)
metaDescription: An as-completed appraisal values a home as if finished, from the plans. Here is how lenders use it to size an Arizona construction loan and set your LTV.

shortDefinition: The Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ) is the government office or official with legal power to approve plans, issue permits, and inspect construction in a given area. For a custom home in Arizona, the AHJ is usually your city or county building department.
definitionHtml:
Somebody has the final say on whether your construction meets the rules, and the building and fire codes give that office a name: the Authority Having Jurisdiction, almost always shortened to AHJ. The 2018 International Residential Code (IRC) defines it as the body responsible for enforcing the code and approving equipment, materials, and installations.

In plain terms, the AHJ is whoever signs off on your project. For a custom home in the Phoenix metro, that depends on where your lot sits. Inside city limits, the city building department is the AHJ. In Scottsdale you answer to the City of Scottsdale; in Phoenix, the City of Phoenix. On unincorporated land, like much of Rio Verde or the desert north of Cave Creek, the county is the AHJ, usually Maricopa County Planning and Development or Pinal County.

The AHJ reviews your plans, issues the building permit, sends inspectors at each stage (footing, framing, electrical, final), and grants the certificate of occupancy at the end. The same office enforces setback rules and zoning. Different AHJs read the code a little differently and adopt different amendments, so a detail that passes in Apache Junction may need a change in Fountain Hills.

For example, if you build on a lot straddling two jurisdictions, or annex a parcel into a city mid-project, the AHJ can change, and you confirm which office holds your permit before you pour concrete. Knowing your AHJ early tells you which code edition applies, what fees to expect, and whose inspectors you will meet on site. When we start a build, identifying the correct AHJ is one of the first steps, because every later approval flows through that office.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2018 IRC, Chapter 1 Scope and Administration
  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Obtain a Building Permit
metaDescription: The Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ) is the office that approves plans, issues permits, and inspects your build. Here is who the AHJ is in Arizona.

shortDefinition: A blower door test measures how airtight a home is by mounting a calibrated fan in an exterior doorway, depressurizing the house, and recording the air leakage. Energy codes set a pass threshold in air changes per hour at 50 pascals (ACH50), commonly 3 to 5 ACH50.
definitionHtml:
A technician seals a calibrated, variable-speed fan into an open exterior doorway with an adjustable panel, then runs the fan to either pull air out of the house or push it in. This is how a blower door test measures how much air leaks through a home's envelope. That fan action creates a pressure difference between inside and outside, usually 50 pascals, and the equipment reads how hard the fan must work to hold that pressure. A leaky house needs a lot of airflow; a tight house needs very little.

The result is reported as air changes per hour at 50 pascals, written ACH50: how many times the home's full volume of air leaks out in an hour under that test pressure. Lower is tighter. The International Energy Conservation Code (IECC) requires a blower door test on new homes and sets a maximum, commonly 3 to 5 ACH50 depending on the code edition and climate zone the local jurisdiction has adopted. The test also helps a technician find the actual leaks, often with a smoke pencil or infrared camera while the fan runs.

For example, on a new Gilbert custom home, the rater runs the blower door near the end of construction. If the house tests at 6 ACH50 and the adopted code caps it at 5, the builder seals top plates, can-light penetrations, and rim joists, then retests until it passes. In Arizona's heat, a tighter envelope means the air conditioner moves less hot outdoor air and runs less.

A blower door test measures air leakage, not insulation value. Insulation is rated by R-value and slows heat conduction; air sealing stops air movement. A home can have great insulation and still fail the blower door if it leaks. Both are needed, and the test is how the air-tightness half gets verified.

Sources

  • U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Saver: Blower Door Tests
  • ENERGY STAR, Seal and Insulate: Identify Problems You Want to Fix
metaDescription: A blower door test measures a home's air leakage with a calibrated fan. Here is how it works, the ACH50 limits energy codes set, and why it matters in Arizona.

shortDefinition: Blue Stake, now called Arizona 811, is the free statewide service that locates and marks buried utility lines before anyone digs. You file a locate request, utilities mark their underground gas, power, water, and communication lines, and you can dig safely without striking them.
definitionHtml:
Blue Stake is the old name for Arizona 811, the statewide one-call service that marks buried utility lines before excavation begins. The service is free. You file a locate request, called a ticket, and each utility with lines on the site sends a locator to spray-paint or flag where its underground gas, electric, water, sewer, and communication lines run. The name comes from the marking system, and color codes signal what is buried where.

Arizona law makes this mandatory, not optional. Under A.R.S. 40-360.21, anyone who plans to excavate must notify Arizona 811 at least two full working days before digging, and cannot start until the lines are marked. You call 811 or file online, the request goes out to the affected utilities, and they respond within that window. Skipping the call and hitting a gas or power line is dangerous and expensive: the law allows fines up to thousands of dollars per violation, plus the cost of the repair.

For example, before excavating footings or trenching a water service on a new build-on-your-lot home in Apache Junction, the excavator files an Arizona 811 ticket. The gas, electric, and water providers mark their lines across the property, and the crew digs around them by hand near the marks.

A locate is different from an easement. Blue Stake tells you where lines physically sit so you do not hit them; an easement is the legal right that lets a utility have lines on your land in the first place. The two often line up, since marked lines frequently run inside a recorded utility easement. On any custom home site, the 811 request is a required step before the first shovel goes in the ground.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 40-360.21 (Underground facilities; excavation; notice)
  • Arizona Corporation Commission, Underground Facilities Law (AZ811)
metaDescription: Blue Stake (Arizona 811) marks buried utility lines before you dig. Here is how the free locate process and Arizona's two-day notice law work for home sites.

shortDefinition: A braced wall panel is a section of wall built and fastened to resist sideways forces from wind and earthquakes, keeping a home from racking or collapsing. The IRC, in Section R602.10, sets where panels go, how wide they must be, and which bracing methods qualify.
definitionHtml:
Without bracing, a wood-framed box can rack under a side load, leaning and deforming into a parallelogram the way an unbraced bookshelf tips over. A braced wall panel is the fix: a specific length of exterior or interior wall built to take lateral loads, the sideways pushes from wind and earthquakes, and transfer them down into the foundation. Braced wall panels stiffen the structure so it stays square and standing.

The 2021 International Residential Code (IRC) governs them in Section R602.10. The code groups panels into braced wall lines (rows of bracing across the building) and prescribes how far apart those lines can be, the minimum width of each panel, and the approved bracing methods. Common methods include let-in diagonal bracing, structural wood sheathing nailed to a set pattern (method WSP), and continuous portal frames around wide openings like a garage door. The code ties the required amount of bracing to the home's wind speed, seismic category, number of stories, and wall length.

For example, a two-story home in the East Valley with a wide front garage opening often uses a continuous-sheathed portal frame on each side of that opening to satisfy R602.10, because the narrow wall sections beside a large door would not brace enough on their own. The plans show each braced wall line, and inspectors verify the nailing and hold-downs before drywall covers them.

A braced wall panel is not the same as a header, which carries the vertical load over an opening. A header handles weight bearing down; a braced wall panel handles forces pushing sideways. Both can sit in the same wall, and a home needs both to be safe. Arizona's relatively low seismic demand still requires wind bracing, so R602.10 applies to nearly every framed home in the state.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2021 IRC Chapter 6 (Wall Construction, Section R602.10 Wall Bracing)
  • International Code Council, codes.iccsafe.org code library
metaDescription: A braced wall panel resists wind and seismic loads in a wood-framed home. Here is how IRC R602.10 defines panels, the methods allowed, and how they work in AZ.

shortDefinition: Build on your lot is a custom home approach where you already own (or buy) the land, then hire a builder to construct a home on that specific lot. Unlike a tract subdivision, the home is designed and permitted for your parcel, its soil, slope, and views, rather than a builder-chosen site.
definitionHtml:
Instead of buying a finished house on land a builder picked, you own the parcel and the builder designs and constructs a home for that exact site. That is the model behind build on your lot: the homeowner controls the land, and the builder builds to it. It is the standard path for a true custom home in Arizona, common in Rio Verde, Cave Creek, Carefree, and the larger desert lots around the Phoenix metro.

The process changes because the land comes first. Before design locks in, the builder checks zoning and setbacks, pulls the recorded plat and its easements, and orders a soil report so the foundation suits that ground. Site conditions such as slope, drainage, rock, well and septic needs, and utility distance all shape the budget and the plan in a way they never do on a tract lot. The builder must hold an Arizona Registrar of Contractors license, and the home is permitted through the county or city for that specific address.

For example, two clients with the same floor plan on different Cave Creek lots can see very different prices, because one lot needs blasting and a long utility run while the other is flat and serviced. The lot, not just the house, drives the cost.

Build on your lot differs from buying a spec home, a finished house a builder put up on speculation, and from a tract home chosen off a community map. Here the land is yours and the home is engineered for it. It also asks more of the owner up front: confirming the lot is buildable, with usable access, utilities, and sound soil. We start a build-on-your-lot project with that feasibility work, because the lot decides what the home can be.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors
  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Residential Construction
metaDescription: Build on your lot means hiring a builder to construct a home on land you own. Here is how the process works for custom homes in Arizona and what to expect.

shortDefinition: Builders risk insurance is a short-term property policy that covers a building while it is under construction. Also called course of construction insurance, it protects the structure and on-site materials from losses like fire, wind, theft, and vandalism until the project is finished and a homeowners policy takes over.
definitionHtml:
A half-built house and a pallet of lumber sitting in the yard are not covered by a standard homeowners policy, which is why every construction project needs its own temporary property policy: builders risk insurance. The Insurance Information Institute also calls it a course of construction policy and notes it "may be available as a stand-alone policy, or as an add-on to your homeowners policy." It covers the partly built structure and the materials on the lot against losses like fire, monsoon wind and rain, theft of supplies, and vandalism. When the home is finished and occupied, the builders risk policy ends and a standard homeowners policy takes over.

How the coverage amount works

The coverage tracks the home's value as it rises. The Insurance Information Institute notes that a building under construction "has an ever-increasing value as more of it is completed." So the policy is usually written to the home's completed value. If a loss hits when the home is half built, a replacement-value policy pays about half of the finished value. A home three-fourths done is covered for three-fourths of its value. The coverage grows as the home does.

What it does not cover

Builders risk is property coverage. It leaves out the contractor's tools and equipment. It also does not cover liability if someone is hurt on the job site. Contractors carry general liability for that. Flood and earthquake are normally excluded too, just as on a homeowners policy. An Arizona lot in a wash or flood zone may need a separate endorsement.

How it is used in Arizona

On an Arizona construction loan, the lender requires a builders risk policy, with the lender named, before it releases the first draw. Either the owner or the builder can carry it, and the contract should say which. The Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions can confirm an insurer or agent is licensed in the state. For the full picture, see our FAQ on what builders risk insurance covers and who pays for it .

Sources

  • Insurance Information Institute: Insurance for remodeling your home (builders risk / course of construction)
  • Insurance Information Institute: Insurance coverage for construction contractors (builders risk valuation)
  • Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions: Homeowners Insurance consumer information

shortDefinition: A casita is a small, separate living space on the same lot as a main house, common in the Southwest. It usually has its own entrance and bathroom, and often a kitchenette, and is used for guests, family, or as a home office. In Arizona it may count as an accessory dwelling unit.
definitionHtml:
A casita, Spanish for "little house," is a compact secondary living unit on the same property as the main home. It is a Southwest term for what zoning codes call an accessory dwelling unit (ADU) or guest house. A casita typically has a private entrance, a bathroom, and often a kitchenette or full kitchen, so a person can live there with some independence from the main house.

People use casitas in many ways: a guest suite, a place for aging parents or adult kids, a home office, a short-term rental where local rules allow, or a pool house. A casita can be detached, sitting as its own small building in the yard, or attached, joined to the main home but with a separate outside door. The right setup depends on the lot and the family's needs.

Building one is a zoning question first. Size limits, whether a kitchen is allowed, parking, and especially setbacks all govern where a detached casita can sit on the lot. Arizona cities set their own ADU rules under state law, and they vary. Phoenix, Scottsdale, and Maricopa County each have different limits on casita size and use.

For example, a Scottsdale custom home with a wide rear yard might add a detached 600-square-foot casita with a bedroom, bath, and kitchenette for visiting family, placed to clear the rear and side setbacks. A casita is often a custom-build feature rather than something found in a finished spec home. We treat it as its own small project, with its own permit, plumbing, and finish allowances inside the main contract.

Sources

  • Arizona State Legislature, A.R.S. Title 9 (accessory dwelling units / municipal zoning)
  • City of Phoenix Planning & Development, Accessory Dwelling Units
metaDescription: A casita is a small detached or attached living space beside a main home. Here is how casitas work, and how they are zoned and permitted on Arizona lots.

shortDefinition: CC&Rs are the Covenants, Conditions & Restrictions: recorded rules that govern what owners can do with their lots in a subdivision or HOA. They cover home size, materials, colors, setbacks, and approvals, and they are legally binding on every owner. In Arizona they fall under planned community law.
definitionHtml:
CC&Rs stands for Covenants, Conditions & Restrictions, the private rulebook recorded against the lots in a subdivision or homeowners association. They are a legal contract that runs with the land: when you buy a lot inside a planned community, you agree to follow its CC&Rs, and so does every owner after you. They sit on top of city zoning, so a home has to satisfy both.

For someone building a custom home, the CC&Rs often control more than the city does. They commonly set a minimum and maximum house size, allowed materials and roof types, exterior colors, fence and wall heights, where outbuildings and casitas can go, and how long construction may take. Many require a design review board (an architectural committee) to approve your plans before you pull a permit. In Arizona, planned communities and their governing documents are defined under the Planned Communities Act in Arizona Revised Statutes Title 33, Chapter 16.

For example, a custom lot in a gated Rio Verde or Carefree community may carry CC&Rs requiring at least 3,000 square feet of living space, earth-tone stucco, a tile roof, and committee sign-off on the elevations before the county will even review the plans. Miss those rules and the HOA can force changes or fines.

CC&Rs are different from zoning, which is the city or county's public law, and from the plat, which is the recorded map of the lots. CC&Rs are the private rules layered on top. They are also enforced by the HOA, not the building department. We read the CC&Rs and clear architectural review at the start of design, because they can override an otherwise legal plan and reshape the whole home.

Sources

  • Arizona Revised Statutes 33-1801 (Planned Communities; applicability)
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 33-1802 (Planned Communities; definitions)
metaDescription: CC&Rs are recorded Covenants, Conditions & Restrictions that govern lots in an HOA or subdivision. Here is how CC&Rs affect building a custom home in Arizona.

shortDefinition: A Certificate of Occupancy (CO) is the document your city or county issues stating that a building is complete, meets code, and is legally safe to live in. In Arizona you cannot move into a new home until the local building department grants the CO.
definitionHtml:
No new building may be occupied until the building official issues one, according to the building code (the 2018 International Residential Code, Section R110). That document is the Certificate of Occupancy, or CO, the official green light to live in or use a building. It is the legal proof that your home passed its final inspections and meets the code in force when the permit was pulled.

You get a CO at the very end of construction, after every required inspection passes: footing, framing, plumbing, electrical, mechanical, and the final walkthrough. The AHJ, your city or county building department, sends an inspector for the final check. When the inspector confirms the home is safe, weather-tight, and complete, the office issues the CO. Only then can you legally move in, set up utilities permanently, or close on a sale.

For example, a custom home in Gilbert might pass framing and electrical but fail the final because the smoke detectors are not hardwired or the address numbers are not posted. The builder fixes those items, calls for re-inspection, and the CO follows. Lenders often hold the last draw until the CO is in hand, so it also affects when the final payment releases.

Some jurisdictions issue a Temporary Certificate of Occupancy (TCO) when the home is livable but a few minor items remain, such as final landscaping or a punch-list repair. A TCO lets you move in with a deadline to finish. We treat the CO as the true finish line of a build, because until the local office signs it, the home is not legally done no matter how complete it looks.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2018 IRC, Chapter 1 Scope and Administration (Section R110, Certificate of Occupancy)
  • City of Phoenix Planning & Development
metaDescription: A Certificate of Occupancy (CO) is the document confirming a home is code-compliant and legal to occupy. Here is how and when you get a CO in Arizona.

shortDefinition: A change order is a written, signed amendment to a construction contract that adds, removes, or changes work and adjusts the price and schedule to match. It documents what changed, what it costs, and how it affects the timeline, so both the owner and builder agree before the new work begins.
definitionHtml:
When work is added, dropped, or altered after your construction contract is signed, that shift gets captured in a change order, a written record of exactly what is different, the new cost, and any change to the completion date. Both the owner and the builder sign it before the work happens, so the original contract stays accurate as the project evolves.

Change orders come up on almost every custom home. You might upgrade from the allowance tile to a pricier one, move a wall, add a casita, or hit a hidden site condition like rock that needs blasting. Each of those is a change to the agreed scope, and each gets its own change order showing the added or credited dollars. That paper trail is what keeps the final price from drifting without anyone noticing.

For example, on a $750,000 Scottsdale build, a homeowner who decides mid-framing to add a 200-square-foot covered patio would get a change order listing the patio's cost, say $18,000, and any days it adds to the schedule. Once signed, that amount folds into the contract and the draw schedule.

In Arizona, the Registrar of Contractors (ROC) licenses home builders and handles disputes, and unclear or undocumented changes are a common source of complaints. A signed change order protects everyone by putting the agreement in writing. A change order is different from an allowance, which is a budget placeholder set at signing; a change order is the formal record when reality differs from the original plan. We require a signed change order before extra work starts, so there are no surprise costs at the end.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors (licensing, contracts, and complaints)
  • U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, Owning a Home (construction financing)

shortDefinition: A cistern is a large water-storage tank that holds a home's water supply where there is no well or city water main. In Arizona's hauled-water areas, like Rio Verde Foothills, a buried or above-ground cistern is filled by a water-hauling truck and feeds the house through a pump.
definitionHtml:
On properties with no well and no connection to a city water main, a house cannot draw water on demand, so it stores a reserve in a cistern, a big storage tank, and draws from that. A pump pressurizes the water from the cistern into the home's plumbing, the same way a well pump would. Cisterns can be buried (below frost-free desert ground) or set above grade, and residential sizes commonly run from a few thousand gallons up to 10,000 gallons or more.

In the Phoenix metro, cisterns are most associated with hauled-water areas. Rio Verde Foothills is the well-known case: many homes there have no well and no pipe to a utility, so a water-hauling truck periodically fills a cistern, and the household lives off that stored water until the next delivery. The cistern size is sized to the household's usage and how often a truck can come, with bigger tanks meaning fewer deliveries.

The cistern choice has consequences beyond storage. Lenders care about it. A home that depends on hauled water into a cistern can be harder to finance, and federal programs are cautious: HUD's individual-water-systems guidance treats cistern and hauled-water supplies as a special case for FHA-insured loans, which is why buyers in these areas confirm their financing path early.

For example, a Rio Verde owner without a well might install a 10,000-gallon buried cistern, contract with a water hauler, and budget for both the tank and recurring delivery costs as part of the true cost of living on that lot.

If you are building where there is no well or main, the cistern is core infrastructure, not an add-on. Size it for real usage and delivery frequency, plan the pump and pressurization with the plumber, and verify how the stored-water supply affects both your loan and your insurance.

Sources

  • HUD, Individual Water Systems (cisterns and FHA financing)
  • U.S. Dept. of Energy FEMP, Rainwater Harvesting Systems (storage tanks and pumps)

shortDefinition: A conditioned attic, also called an unvented attic, is an attic sealed and insulated at the roofline instead of the ceiling, so it sits inside the home's cooled envelope. This keeps ductwork and the air handler out of extreme heat, which improves efficiency in hot climates like Arizona.
definitionHtml:
In a normal attic, the builder insulates the attic floor (the ceiling of the rooms below) and vents the attic to outside air. A conditioned attic does the opposite: the builder seals the attic airtight and insulates at the roofline instead, on the underside of the roof deck, bringing the space inside the home's thermal envelope. The attic is then unvented, which is why it is also called an unvented attic. It stays much closer to the temperature of the living space.

The reason to do this is ductwork. In a standard vented attic, the air handler and ducts sit in air that can pass 140 degrees in a Phoenix July. The U.S. Department of Energy notes that running HVAC in vented attics raises heating and cooling energy use by about 10% from duct losses alone. Move the attic inside the envelope and that loss largely goes away, because the ducts now run through cooled space. The PNNL Building America Solution Center describes sealing the attic "completely within the building thermal envelope." Builders usually do this with spray foam or rigid foam at the roof deck, per 2018 IRC Section R806.5.

For example, a custom home in Rio Verde with the HVAC system in the attic can be built with closed-cell spray foam under the roof deck. The attic becomes a semi-conditioned space near room temperature, so the cooled air in the ducts no longer fights a superheated attic. Cooling bills drop and the equipment lasts longer in the milder environment.

A conditioned attic is not just "more insulation." It changes where the thermal boundary sits, moving it from the ceiling up to the roof. Because the attic is sealed, the design must control moisture for the climate, which is why Arizona builders detail these assemblies carefully rather than simply spraying foam and walking away.

Sources

  • PNNL Building America Solution Center, Unvented Conditioned Attic with Spray Foam Below Roof Deck
  • U.S. Department of Energy, Building America: Unvented, Conditioned Attics (Top Innovation)

shortDefinition: Conditioned space is the part of a home that is heated and cooled and sits inside the insulated, air-sealed building envelope. It excludes areas like a vented attic, an unconditioned garage, or a crawlspace. The boundary decides where insulation and air-sealing go.
definitionHtml:
Every home has a line where the HVAC system's reach stops, and that line defines conditioned space: the part of the house that is heated, cooled, or both, sitting inside the thermal envelope. The envelope is the insulated, air-sealed boundary that separates indoor air from the outdoors. Bedrooms, living rooms, kitchens, and most hallways are conditioned. A vented attic, an unconditioned garage, an open patio, and a crawlspace are not. They sit outside the boundary.

The line between conditioned and unconditioned space matters because it tells builders where the insulation and the air barrier go. They belong on the boundary of the conditioned space, not on just any wall or ceiling. Get the boundary wrong and you can insulate a surface that holds no living space while leaving a real heat leak somewhere else.

The line also drives the Manual J cooling load and the equipment that serves it. Only conditioned floor area counts in the calculation that sizes the air conditioner. In Arizona, a common choice is to bring the attic and ducts inside the envelope by insulating at the roofline. The ducts no longer bake in a 140-degree attic. For example, that one move can shift a Mesa home's ducts from unconditioned to conditioned and cut cooling loss sharply.

Conditioned space is not the same as "finished" space. A finished bonus room over a garage is conditioned only if it is inside the sealed envelope and served by the HVAC system, sometimes by a dedicated mini-split. Knowing where the conditioned boundary runs is the start of an efficient, comfortable desert home.

Sources

  • U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Saver (insulation and the thermal envelope)
  • ENERGY STAR, Seal and Insulate (the building envelope)

shortDefinition: A construction defect is a flaw in the design, materials, or workmanship of a home that makes part of it fail to work as it should, such as a leaking roof, cracked foundation, or faulty wiring. In Arizona, owners must follow a notice process under the Purchaser Dwelling Act before suing.
definitionHtml:
A foundation that cracks from soil movement, a roof or window that leaks, plumbing that fails, stucco that separates, wiring that is not to code: each is a construction defect, something built or designed wrong in a home so that a part of it does not perform the way it should. It can come from a bad design, defective materials, or poor workmanship. A defect is about a failure to meet the standard of a sound, code-compliant home, not just a cosmetic preference.

In Arizona, defect claims run through the Purchaser Dwelling Act in Arizona Revised Statutes Title 12. Before an owner can sue a builder over a defect, the law requires the owner to send written notice describing the problem and give the builder a chance to inspect and offer to repair it. The builder can respond by fixing the issue, paying, or declining. This notice-and-repair step is mandatory, and skipping it can stall or sink a lawsuit. Separate time limits also apply for how long after completion a claim can be brought.

For example, if a homeowner in Queen Creek finds drywall cracks and sticking doors two years in from expansive-soil heave, the path is to notify the builder in writing under the Act, not to file suit first. That gives the builder the legal opening to inspect and cure.

A construction defect is different from a punch list item, which is a small unfinished or cosmetic task fixed at the end of the job before closing. A defect is a real failure of performance or code, often surfacing later. We build to engineered details and code to avoid defects, and we stand behind the work, but the Act's notice-and-repair process is the right first move if a real problem appears.

Sources

  • Arizona Revised Statutes 12-1361 (Purchaser Dwelling Actions; definitions)
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 12-1362 (Notice and opportunity to repair)

shortDefinition: A contractor's license is the state credential, issued by the Arizona Registrar of Contractors, that lets a person legally build or remodel for pay. Arizona requires it for almost all construction over $1,000, and the license class (such as B for residential general contracting) sets what work the holder may perform.
definitionHtml:
Think of it as the legal permission slip to build for money in Arizona. The Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) issues it, and A.R.S. 32-1101 defines who needs one: anyone who, for pay, builds, alters, repairs, adds to, or moves a structure. A.R.S. 32-1151 then makes it unlawful to act as a contractor without that license. The practical trigger is the $1,000 rule. Work where the total price (labor plus materials) is more than $1,000, or that needs a building permit, requires a licensed contractor.

The license is not one size. The ROC sorts licenses into classes that limit what each holder can do. A custom-home builder typically holds a B (General Residential Contractor) license, which covers building a complete house and managing the trades under it. There are also dual (residential and commercial) and specialty classes (for example, plumbing, electrical, or roofing), so the sub who wires your house holds a different class than the general contractor who runs the job.

A real license is more than a card. To get one, a contractor must show qualifying experience, pass trade and business-management exams, and post a license bond (or cash deposit) that protects people the contractor might fail to pay or harm. Residential contractors also fund the Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund. All of that protection only exists if the license exists.

For example, a Scottsdale owner planning a $700,000 build confirms the builder's B license is active on the ROC license lookup, checks the class matches the work, and sees no open complaints, before signing the contract.

The one common exception is the owner-builder path, where you build or improve your own home, live in it, and do not sell it within a year. Outside that narrow lane, hiring an unlicensed contractor in Arizona means no recovery fund, no recourse through the ROC, and a builder who usually cannot legally sue to collect from you.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 32-1101 (Definitions; contractor)
  • A.R.S. 32-1151 (Contracting without a license; prohibition)
  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors, About AZ ROC

shortDefinition: A contractor's license bond is a surety bond (or cash deposit) that Arizona requires a contractor to post with the Registrar of Contractors before getting licensed. It guarantees a pool of money that homeowners, suppliers, and workers can claim against if the contractor breaks the contract or fails to pay.
definitionHtml:
Before Arizona hands a contractor a license, it makes them post a contractor's license bond, the financial backstop that protects the people they work for. Under A.R.S. 32-1152, the contractor buys a surety bond from a bonding company (or leaves a cash deposit with the state treasurer) in an amount the Registrar of Contractors sets. If the contractor violates the contracting laws or breaks the contract, the people they harmed can file a claim against that bond to recover money.

The bond is a three-party promise. The contractor is the principal, the bonding company is the surety, and you (the homeowner, a supplier, or a worker) are the protected party. The contractor pays a premium for the bond, but the bond pays out to claimants, not to the contractor. If the surety pays a claim, it then chases the contractor for repayment, so the bond is not free money for the builder.

Bond amounts in Arizona scale with license type and volume. A.R.S. 32-1152 ties the figure to the work the license covers, and the ROC's schedule runs from roughly $1,000 for small specialty residential work up to $15,000 for residential general contractors, with larger commercial bonds higher. These are deliberately modest, which is the key limit owners miss.

For example, a Fountain Hills homeowner on a $600,000 build cannot expect a $9,000 license bond to cover a six-figure construction failure. That is exactly why residential contractors also pay into the Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund (up to $30,000 per residence) and why owners use lien waivers to keep suppliers from filing a mechanic's lien.

Think of the bond as the first, smallest layer of protection. It deters fly-by-night operators and covers smaller disputes, but on a custom home it is one of several tools, alongside the recovery fund, a clear contract, and ROC complaint rights, that together protect your money.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 32-1152 (Bond or cash deposit; amount; liability)
  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors, Recovery Fund

shortDefinition: A cool roof is a roof built to reflect more sunlight and shed more heat than a standard roof, so it stays cooler in the sun. It uses reflective coatings, tiles, or membranes to lower roof and attic temperatures, which trims summer cooling costs in hot climates like Arizona.
definitionHtml:
Swap a dark, absorbent roof surface for a reflective one and the roof does the same job but runs far cooler in direct sun. That is the whole idea behind a cool roof. It keeps the attic and the rooms below it cooler, so the air conditioner works less during a long Arizona summer.

Two properties make a roof "cool." The first is solar reflectance: how much sunlight the surface bounces away. White roofing reflects about 60% to 90% of sunlight, and even special "cool colored" products reflect roughly 30% to 60%, far more than a dark shingle. The second is thermal emittance: how readily the surface gives off the heat it does absorb. The U.S. Department of Energy notes a conventional roof can hit 150 degrees F or more on a sunny afternoon, while a reflective roof can stay more than 50 degrees F cooler in the same conditions.

For example, a dark tile roof in Casa Grande can radiate that 150-degree heat straight into the attic, where it heats the ductwork and pushes attic temperatures well past comfort. Swapping to a reflective tile, a light "cool" coating, or a reflective underlayment keeps the deck much cooler, so less heat ever reaches the ceiling and the AC has a lighter load through July and August.

A cool roof is not the same as attic insulation or a radiant barrier, though they all attack heat. The cool roof stops heat at the surface, insulation slows what gets through, and a radiant barrier reflects what radiates into the attic. On a desert custom home, the three work together. The cool roof is the first line of defense against the sun before the heat ever reaches the structure.

Sources

  • U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Saver: Cool Roofs
  • PNNL Building America Solution Center, Unvented Attic Insulation

shortDefinition: A cost-plus contract pays the builder the actual cost of the work (labor, materials, and subcontractors) plus a set fee or percentage for the builder's profit and overhead. The owner sees the real invoices, so the final price is open and moves with the true cost of the job.
definitionHtml:
What you owe under a cost-plus contract breaks into the real money paid to trades, suppliers, and labor, plus an agreed markup for running the job. The "cost" side is the documented, actual spend. The "plus" side is the builder's fee, set as either a fixed dollar amount or a percentage of cost (often somewhere around 10 to 20 percent). You pay the documented cost and the fee on top, and the builder shows you the invoices.

The defining feature is transparency. In a cost-plus job the owner can see exactly what the concrete sub charged, what the windows cost, and what the framing crew was paid, because the contract is built on passing those real costs through. That openness is also the trade-off: because the final number follows the true cost of the work, it is not locked in at signing. If material prices rise or the design grows, the total rises with it.

Compare that to the two other common structures. A fixed-price (lump-sum) contract sets one price up front and the builder absorbs overruns, which gives the owner certainty but bakes in a contingency cushion and hides the line-item costs. A guaranteed maximum price (GMP) is the hybrid: cost-plus transparency, but with a cap the total cannot exceed.

For example, a Scottsdale owner builds a highly custom home where finishes are still being chosen. Cost-plus lets the work start before every selection is locked, with the owner paying real costs as decisions firm up, rather than forcing the builder to guess a lump sum and pad it.

Arizona requires a written residential contract with specific terms under A.R.S. 32-1158, and a good cost-plus contract spells out the fee, what counts as a reimbursable cost, how allowances and change orders are handled, and how often you get invoices. Cost-plus rewards trust and active owner involvement. If you want a fixed number above all, a fixed-price or GMP contract fits better.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 32-1158 (Residential contracts; required terms)
  • U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, What is a construction loan?

shortDefinition: Defensible space is the managed buffer between a home and the wildland around it, cleared and thinned to slow a wildfire and protect the structure from embers, flames, and heat. Arizona organizes it into zones measured outward from the house, typically reaching up to 100 feet.
definitionHtml:
Clearing and thinning the area around a home so a wildfire has less fuel to carry flames to the structure is what creates defensible space. It is not a fire break of bare dirt. It is a managed landscape where vegetation is spaced, pruned, and kept low enough that an approaching fire drops to the ground, slows down, and gives the home, and firefighters, a fighting chance.

Arizona's Department of Forestry and Fire Management organizes defensible space into zones measured outward from the house. Zone 0, the first 0 to 5 feet, is the ember-resistant zone: no bark mulch, no woodpiles, no flammable plants tight against the walls. Zone 1 runs out to about 30 feet, where you remove dead plants, keep grass low, and break up continuous shrubs. Zone 2 extends from 30 feet to roughly 100 feet (or the property line), thinned so tree canopies do not touch and a ground fire cannot ladder up into the trees. Embers, not the wall of flame, destroy most homes, which is why Zone 0 right against the structure matters most.

For example, a custom home on acreage in Cave Creek or the Rio Verde foothills, where desert brush meets the building pad, needs that 100-foot graduated buffer: gravel and hardscape near the walls, spaced low plantings out to 30 feet, and thinned native vegetation beyond.

Defensible space connects to a few other rules. It is distinct from a setback, which is a zoning distance from a property line, though the two can overlap. CC&Rs or an HOA may dictate what landscaping is allowed, so wildfire clearing has to fit those. Arizona's Firewise USA communities apply these zone standards neighborhood-wide, and on wildland-adjacent lots we plan the grading and landscape with that buffer built in from the start.

Sources

  • Arizona Department of Forestry and Fire Management (DFFM), Fire
  • Arizona DFFM, Firewise USA

shortDefinition: A Design Review Committee (DRC), also called an Architectural Review Committee (ARC), is the HOA body that approves a custom home's design before you build. It checks your plans against the community's private guidelines, separate from the city. In Arizona, A.R.S. 33-1817 sets rules for how it must operate.
definitionHtml:
In a gated or master-planned community, you cannot start a custom home until a private committee inside your homeowners association signs off on your design, and that committee is the Design Review Committee (DRC), often called an Architectural Review Committee (ARC). It checks your plans against the community's recorded CC&Rs and design guidelines: height, setbacks, materials, roof type, exterior colors, square footage, walls, and landscaping. It approves, approves with conditions, or rejects.

The DRC is a private body, not a government one, and that is the key difference from city plan review. The city or county building department checks whether your home is safe and legal under the building code and zoning. The DRC checks whether your home fits the community's look. The two are separate approvals, and you usually need both. A design that passes the county can still be told by the DRC to change its roof tile, paint color, or height. The DRC has no power over code or safety; the building department has no power over private design taste.

Arizona law sets rules for how a DRC must operate in a planned community that has adopted design guidelines. A.R.S. 33-1817 requires the committee to "hold a final design approval meeting for the purpose of issuing approval of the plans," which you may attend. It requires any construction deposit to sit in a trust account, and it calls for at least two on-site reviews during construction. The same law makes clear that DRC approval "shall not constitute a representation or warranty that the plans or construction comply with applicable governmental requirements," so the committee's sign-off never replaces the city permit.

For example, a custom home in Troon North goes to that community's Architectural Review Committee, which requires a registered Arizona architect, reviews the building envelope, and runs site inspections during the build, all set out in the Troon North design guidelines . The owner still pulls a separate City of Scottsdale permit through city plan review. We clear the DRC and the city in parallel, designing to the community's guidelines from day one so neither approval stalls the build. For the government side, see our plan review entry .

Sources

  • A.R.S. 33-1817 (Design, architectural committees; review; final approval meeting)
  • Troon North Association Architectural Standards (Architectural Review Committee process)

shortDefinition: A draw schedule is the payment plan in a construction contract that releases money in stages as the home reaches set milestones, such as foundation, framing, and drywall. Each release is a draw. It ties payments to completed work rather than paying everything up front.
definitionHtml:
Instead of paying the full price at the start or only at the end, the owner (or the construction lender) pays the builder in chunks called draws, each one released when the project hits an agreed milestone. A draw schedule is what lists those milestones and the dollar amount or percentage tied to each.

A typical schedule for an Arizona custom home releases money at signing (a limited deposit), then after the foundation pour, framing, dry-in, and drywall, with a final payment at the certificate of occupancy. Each draw is verified before it pays out. On a construction loan, the lender sends an inspector to confirm the milestone is truly done. This protects everyone. The owner does not pay ahead of the work, and the builder gets paid as real progress lands.

For example, a $700,000 build might draw 20% at framing complete. The builder finishes framing, the inspection confirms it, and that draw releases to cover the work already done and the next phase's costs. Lenders commonly hold back the final draw until the home passes its final inspection and the certificate of occupancy is issued.

In Arizona, the Registrar of Contractors limits how large a down payment a home contractor can require. That is one reason the rest of the price is split into a draw schedule. A draw schedule is not the same as an allowance, which budgets the cost of an undecided finish. The draw schedule sets when money moves. An allowance sets how much a given item costs. We build clear, milestone-based draw schedules so clients always know what they are paying for and when.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors, Contracts and Down Payment Limits
  • U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, Construction Loans

shortDefinition: Dust control training is a certification Maricopa County requires for people responsible for dust on a construction site under Air Quality Rule 310. Larger permitted sites must keep a trained dust control coordinator on site during dust-generating work, and certifications are valid for three years.
definitionHtml:
Because the Phoenix metro is a federal dust (PM-10) nonattainment area, Maricopa County Air Quality requires certain construction site staff to hold dust control training, a certification under Rule 310, the county's fugitive-dust rule. The rule exists so site staff are trained in how to keep dust out of the air during earthwork. You take the training through the county's online learning system. All dust control certifications are valid for three years from the date they are issued.

Who needs it depends on the role and the size of the site. All water truck and water-pull drivers, including subcontractors, must complete at least basic training. On sites with more than one acre but less than five acres of disturbed surface, the site superintendent must complete Rule 310 Basic training. On sites with five or more acres of disturbed surface, the rule goes further. The site must keep a dust control coordinator on site at all times during dust-generating operations. That coordinator must complete 310 Comprehensive training. A separate rule, Rule 316, sets its own training tiers for sources like sand and gravel operations.

For a custom-home owner, the takeaway is simple. The training duty rides with whoever is responsible for dust on the lot. That is usually your builder or grading contractor. If you act as your own general contractor on a large lot, the duty can land on you. The training pairs with the dust control permit , which is required once you disturb 0.10 acre. It also fits the broader pre-construction permit layer of dust, stormwater, and grading approvals.

For example, a builder grading a six-acre Cave Creek lot must keep a 310-Comprehensive coordinator on site whenever dirt is moving. The water-truck driver wetting the soil must hold at least basic certification.

Because training tiers, the acreage breakpoints, and certification rules can change, confirm the current Rule 310 training requirements with Maricopa County Air Quality before you mobilize.

Sources

shortDefinition: A dwelling action is the legal name in Arizona for a lawsuit a homeowner brings against the builder, developer, or contractor over a construction defect in a home. It is defined in A.R.S. 12-1361 and triggers a required notice-and-repair process before suit.
definitionHtml:
When a homeowner sues the people who built or sold a home over an alleged construction defect, Arizona law has a specific name for that lawsuit: a dwelling action. The term comes from the Purchaser Dwelling Act in Arizona Revised Statutes Title 12. A.R.S. 12-1361 defines a dwelling action as a court proceeding or arbitration by a buyer against the seller, builder, developer, or contractor over a defect in the design, construction, or sale of a dwelling.

What makes a dwelling action special is that the homeowner cannot simply file suit. The Act, in A.R.S. 12-1362 and the surrounding sections, requires the owner to first send the builder written notice describing each claimed defect, then give the builder a chance to inspect and to offer to repair, replace, or pay. Only after that notice-and-opportunity-to-repair process plays out can the dwelling action proceed in court. Skipping the notice can get the case delayed or dismissed.

For example, if a homeowner in Buckeye discovers recurring stucco cracks and a leaking window two years after move-in, the formal path is a dwelling action under the Act: serve the written defect notice, allow the inspection, and see whether the builder offers a fix before any court filing. The statute defines the choreography.

A dwelling action is the lawsuit; a construction defect is the underlying problem that the lawsuit is about. The two terms are related but not identical. A dwelling action is also bounded in time by Arizona's statute of repose (A.R.S. 12-552), which caps how many years after the home is finished a claim can be brought. Understanding both is the difference between a claim that proceeds and one that is barred.

Sources

  • Arizona Revised Statutes 12-1361 (Purchaser Dwelling Actions; definitions)
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 12-552 (Actions based on a defect in real property improvement)

shortDefinition: An easement is a legal right for someone else to use part of your land for a specific purpose, such as utility lines, drainage, or access, even though you still own that land. Easements run with the property and usually limit what you can build over them.
definitionHtml:
You can hold the deed to your lot outright and still have a power company running lines across it, a city crew maintaining a sewer under it, or a neighbor crossing it to reach a back parcel. That shared-use right is an easement: the right to use a piece of land you do not own, for a defined purpose. The land stays yours, but you share the use of that strip, and you cannot block the easement holder from doing what the easement allows.

Most residential easements are drawn right on the recorded plat and described in the deed. Common types in Arizona include utility easements along lot edges for electric, gas, water, and cable, drainage easements that carry stormwater across the lot, and access easements that give a landlocked parcel a way to the street. An easement "runs with the land," meaning it stays in force when the property sells, so a new owner takes the lot subject to it.

For example, a Scottsdale lot might carry a 10-foot public utility easement along the rear property line. You own that 10 feet, but you usually cannot build a casita, pool, or permanent structure on it, because crews need clear access to the lines. A pool or footing placed over an easement can be forced out at your cost.

An easement is different from a setback, which is a zoning rule about distance from a line, and from an outright sale of land. With an easement you keep ownership; you just share the use. Before designing a home, we map every easement on the recorded plat, because they shrink the buildable area as surely as setbacks do and quietly decide where the house, garage, and pool can sit.

Sources

  • Cornell Law School, Legal Information Institute: Easement
  • Maricopa County Recorder, Map Recordation Criteria
metaDescription: An easement is a legal right for others to use part of your land for utilities, drainage, or access. Here is how easements affect what you can build in Arizona.

shortDefinition: Expansive soil is clay-rich ground that swells when it absorbs water and shrinks as it dries. This swelling and shrinking pushes on foundations and can crack slabs, walls, and floors. It is common across central Arizona and drives how custom home foundations are designed.
definitionHtml:
Ground with a high clay content changes volume with moisture: that is expansive soil. When the clay takes on water, during monsoon storms, irrigation, or a plumbing leak, it swells and lifts. When it dries out in Arizona's long hot season, it shrinks and pulls away. This up-and-down movement, called heave when it rises, puts uneven pressure on whatever sits on top.

For a home, that pressure is the problem. Expansive soil can crack a concrete slab, split drywall, jam doors, and separate walls from floors. The damage is slow and seasonal, which is why it is easy to miss until it is serious. Central Arizona has plenty of these soils, especially in the agricultural basins around Casa Grande, Queen Creek, and Apache Junction, where clays are deep and land subsidence adds to the risk.

Builders manage expansive soil by testing it first. A geotechnical engineer drills the lot, measures the clay's swell potential, and writes a soils report. That report drives the foundation design, almost always a post-tension slab engineered to the specific site, sometimes with deeper footings, moisture barriers, or imported fill. The AHJ will not approve the permit without this engineering on expansive ground.

For example, two lots a mile apart can need very different foundations if one sits on stable desert caliche and the other on swelling clay. This is why a generic slab design is risky in Arizona. Expansive soil is the main reason foundation engineering here is site-specific, and it is the soil condition that the post-tension slab was built to handle.

Sources

  • Arizona Geological Survey, Problem Soils (expansive clays)
  • U.S. Geological Survey, Environmental Characteristics of Clays and Clay Mineral Deposits
metaDescription: Expansive soil is clay that swells when wet and shrinks when it dries, cracking foundations. Here is how it affects custom home foundations in Arizona.

shortDefinition: Fenestration is the industry term for all the openings in a building's shell that let in light or air: windows, doors, skylights, and glass walls, plus their frames. In energy codes it is where heat enters and escapes, so fenestration ratings like U-factor and SHGC drive a home's efficiency.
definitionHtml:
Builders and energy codes need one word for every glazed or operable opening in a shell, not five separate ones, and that word is fenestration: windows, exterior doors, skylights, and glass walls, plus their frames and glass. The word comes from the Latin for window, but in building and energy code it covers the whole category. If light, air, or a person can pass through a hole in the wall or roof, that opening is fenestration.

The term matters because fenestration is the weak point in a home's shell. You can pack walls and roofs with insulation, but glass and doors move heat much more easily. So codes and ratings treat fenestration on its own. The National Fenestration Rating Council (NFRC) certifies how each product performs. It rates U-factor, solar heat gain coefficient, visible light, air leakage, and condensation. Energy codes like the IRC and IECC set fenestration limits by climate zone, and inspectors check the NFRC label against those limits.

For example, when a custom home in Scottsdale goes through plan review, the fenestration schedule lists every window, door, and skylight with its NFRC U-factor and SHGC. The reviewer confirms those numbers meet the code for Arizona's hot climate zone before the city issues the permit. A wall of west-facing glass looks great. It is also the home's biggest fenestration heat load, so the glass spec has to carry its weight.

Fenestration is broader than "windows." It also covers doors and skylights, and it is the language codes and product labels use. For an Arizona homeowner, the takeaway is simple. The fenestration ratings, not the brand or the look, decide how much desert heat the openings let in.

Sources

  • National Fenestration Rating Council (NFRC), Residential Certification
  • U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Saver: Energy Efficient Windows
metaDescription: Fenestration is all the openings in a building shell: windows, doors, and skylights. Here is how fenestration ratings shape energy code and Arizona homes.

shortDefinition: A final inspection is the last code inspection of a new home, where a city or county inspector confirms the completed build meets the approved plans and the building code. Passing it is what allows the certificate of occupancy to be issued, so you can legally move in.
definitionHtml:
Footing, framing, electrical, plumbing, mechanical: each of those inspections has to pass before the next stage of a build proceeds, and the final inspection is the last one in that chain. An inspector visits the finished house and checks that everything is complete, safe, and built to the approved plans. When it passes, the AHJ issues the certificate of occupancy, the document that lets you legally live in the home.

The 2018 International Residential Code requires a final inspection after the project is finished and before the building is used. The inspector confirms the work matches the permitted plans and the code. They check that smoke and carbon-monoxide alarms work, stairs and railings are safe, electrical panels and outlets are correct, plumbing fixtures function, and required safety items like emergency egress windows and address numbers are in place.

For example, a custom home in Fountain Hills schedules its final inspection once the house is move-in ready. If the inspector finds an open item, like a missing handrail or an unfinished GFCI outlet, the inspection fails and the builder corrects it before a re-inspection. Each jurisdiction sets its own scheduling window, often a day or two out.

A final inspection is not the same as the builder's punch list, which is the owner-and-builder list of cosmetic touch-ups. The final inspection is about code and safety, not paint scuffs or cabinet adjustments. It is also separate from any HOA or warranty walk-through. We line up the final inspection only when the home is genuinely complete, because a failed final inspection delays your certificate of occupancy and your move-in date.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2018 IRC, Chapter 1 Scope and Administration (inspections and certificate of occupancy)
  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Obtain a Building Permit
metaDescription: A final inspection is the last code check on a new home before move-in. Here is what inspectors look for in Arizona and how it leads to your occupancy.

shortDefinition: A footing is the widened concrete base at the bottom of a foundation that spreads the home's weight onto the soil below. It sits under walls, posts, and grade beams, keeping the structure from sinking or settling unevenly. Footing size depends on the load above and the soil's bearing strength.
definitionHtml:
Every wall, column, and grade beam sends its load down into the ground, and something has to spread that weight across enough soil to carry it. That job belongs to the footing, a wider slab or strip of concrete poured at the base of the structure above it. Without that spread, the weight would punch into the dirt and the home would settle, crack, or tilt.

Footing size comes from two numbers: how much load is pressing down, and how much weight the soil can safely hold (its bearing capacity). The International Residential Code sets minimum footing widths and depths in Chapter 4, and a soils report can require more. Heavier loads or weaker soil mean a wider, deeper footing. The footing also has to reach below any zone that moves with frost or moisture, though in the low desert frost depth is shallow and the bigger concern is expansive clay.

For example, a two-story custom home in Cave Creek puts more load on its perimeter than a single-story ranch, so its continuous wall footings are wider, and an interior steel post may sit on its own thickened pad footing sized for that point load. The structural engineer calculates each one.

A footing is not the same as a stem wall, which is the short wall that rises from the footing to support the floor above, or a grade beam, which spans between supports. The footing is the part that actually contacts and loads the soil. On Arizona's expansive soils, footing design ties directly to the geotechnical report, because the same load needs more support on swelling clay than on firm caliche. We pour footings to the engineer's stamped sizes, never to a generic detail.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2021 IRC Chapter 4 (Foundations)
  • Arizona Geological Survey
metaDescription: A footing is the widened concrete base that spreads a home's weight onto the soil. Here is how footings work and how they are sized on Arizona foundations.

shortDefinition: A grade beam is a reinforced concrete beam that runs along or just below ground level and carries the load of the walls above, spanning between footings, piers, or stronger soil. It lets a foundation bridge over weak or expansive soil instead of resting directly on it.
definitionHtml:
Instead of letting a wall load press straight down into the soil everywhere along its length, a grade beam acts like a bridge: a concrete beam, reinforced with steel, that sits at or near ground level (the grade) and collects the wall's weight, delivering it to spaced supports such as footings, drilled piers, or pads founded on reliable soil.

Grade beams matter most where the surface soil cannot be trusted. On expansive clay that swells and shrinks with moisture, a beam stiff enough to span the soft spots keeps the wall straight even as the ground moves underneath. In a post-tension slab foundation, thickened grade beams run through the slab in a grid, stiffening the whole foundation into one plate that resists the soil's push and pull. The International Residential Code covers their reinforcement and depth in its foundation chapter, and a soils engineer sizes them to the site.

For example, a custom home in Casa Grande on deep expansive clay may sit on a system of grade beams tied to drilled piers that reach down to firmer soil. The beams carry the walls, the piers carry the beams, and the swelling surface clay is taken out of the load path.

A grade beam differs from a plain footing, which simply spreads a load onto the soil directly beneath it, and from a stem wall, which lifts the structure above grade. The grade beam's defining trait is that it spans, transferring load sideways to stronger support. We use grade beams where the geotechnical report calls for them, because skipping that spanning capacity on bad soil is how foundations crack.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2021 IRC Chapter 4 (Foundations)
  • Arizona Geological Survey
metaDescription: A grade beam is a reinforced concrete beam at ground level that carries wall loads between supports. Here is how grade beams work on Arizona foundations.

shortDefinition: Graywater is gently used household wastewater from clothes washers, tubs, showers, and bathroom sinks, but not from toilets, kitchen sinks, or dishwashers. In Arizona, a home system under 400 gallons a day can reuse it for irrigation under ADEQ's Type 1 general permit.
definitionHtml:
Graywater is the wastewater a home makes from washing, not from toilets or the kitchen. It comes from clothes washers, bathtubs, showers, and bathroom sinks. It does not include water from toilets, kitchen sinks, or dishwashers. Those carry grease and food and count as blackwater. Caught separately, graywater can water a yard instead of going to the sewer or septic system.

In Arizona, graywater reuse is run by the Department of Environmental Quality (ADEQ). A typical home falls under the Type 1 Reclaimed Water General Permit for graywater. It covers home systems that make less than 400 gallons per day. The permit is automatic if you follow its rules. You file no application and pay no fee as long as you comply. The rules say to use the water only on your own lot, apply it by drip or flood and never by spray, keep it below the surface or covered, and store it no more than about 24 hours.

For example, a homeowner in Cave Creek can route the laundry and shower drains to a drip line that feeds desert trees, reusing hundreds of gallons a week. In Arizona's dry climate, that reuse stretches a scarce resource and keeps yard plants alive through summer. It also draws less from the well or city supply. Larger flows, between 400 and 3,000 gallons per day, move up to a Type 3 permit.

Graywater is not the same as rainwater harvesting, which catches roof runoff. It is also not treated effluent from a plant. Graywater is your own lightly used indoor water, reused on site. Planning for it early, when you lay out plumbing on a build-on-your-lot project, is far cheaper than adding separate drain lines later.

Sources

  • Arizona Department of Environmental Quality, Using Gray Water at Home
  • Arizona Department of Environmental Quality, Recycled Water (rules and permits)
metaDescription: Graywater is reusable wastewater from washers, showers, and bath sinks. Here is what counts and how Arizona's ADEQ Type 1 graywater permit lets homes reuse it.
