shortDefinition: A guaranteed maximum price (GMP) is a contract where the builder is paid actual costs plus a fee, but the total cannot exceed a hard cap. If the job comes in under the cap, the owner usually keeps the savings; if it runs over, the builder absorbs the overage, not the owner.
definitionHtml:
Cost-plus pricing with a ceiling you cannot go past describes a guaranteed maximum price, usually written GMP. The builder bills actual costs (labor, materials, subs) plus an agreed fee, just like cost-plus, but the contract names a maximum total. That cap is the guarantee: the owner will not pay more than that number for the agreed scope, no matter what the real costs add up to.

The GMP splits risk in a specific way. Below the cap, you pay the documented costs and see the invoices. Above the cap, the builder absorbs the overage rather than passing it to you. Many GMP contracts also include a savings clause, so if the job finishes under the cap, the owner keeps part or all of the difference (sometimes shared with the builder as an incentive). That structure rewards the builder for running an efficient job instead of letting costs drift.

Where a GMP can move is scope. The cap covers the design and selections in the contract. If you add a casita, upgrade to a tile roof, or change the kitchen layout after signing, that is a change order, and a legitimate change order can raise the GMP. The cap protects you from cost overruns on the agreed work, not from new work you ask for.

For example, a Cave Creek owner signs a GMP of $850,000 for a defined custom home. The builder buys lumber that runs higher than estimated, but because total costs land just under the cap, the owner still pays no more than $850,000. Had costs blown past it on the same scope, the builder would have eaten the difference.

A GMP fits owners who want open-book pricing but cannot stomach an uncapped cost-plus number. As with any Arizona residential contract under A.R.S. 32-1158, the cap, the fee, the allowance amounts, and the change-order process should all be in writing before work starts.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 32-1158 (Residential contracts; required terms)
  • U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, What is a construction loan?
metaDescription: A guaranteed maximum price (GMP) caps a cost-plus build. Here is how a GMP contract works, who eats the overage, and how it compares to fixed-price contracts.

shortDefinition: Hauled water is potable water delivered to a home by truck and kept in an onsite storage tank, used where there is no city water line or well. In Rio Verde Foothills, haulers fill at the EPCOR standpipe and deliver to tanks of 2,500 gallons and up that the home runs off.
definitionHtml:
Instead of water arriving through a pipe in the street, hauled water is drinking water brought to a home by truck and stored in a tank on the property, the water source for homes that have no city water line and no well. A hauler fills a tanker truck at a public fill point and delivers it into the home's storage tank, also called a cistern. The house then runs off that tank, drawing water with a pressure pump so faucets and showers work like any other home. You only notice the tank when it runs low and needs a refill.

In Arizona, hauled water is common on rural and unincorporated land, and Rio Verde Foothills is the best-known example. There, with no municipal water, haulers fill at the EPCOR standpipe, a regulated fill station that opened January 1, 2026, and deliver to home tanks that commonly run 2,500 gallons and up. A full-time family home often uses a 5,000 to 10,000 gallon tank, sized to hold two to four weeks of supply so a late delivery never leaves the home dry. The cost has two parts: the per-gallon water rate at the fill station and the hauler's separate delivery fee.

For example, a buyer building on a Rio Verde Foothills lot with a poor well outlook may choose hauled water over drilling. They place a tank on a level pad a delivery truck can reach year-round, open an EPCOR account, and schedule deliveries. The tank, its pad, and the pump all have to fit on the build-on-your-lot site plan and stay within their setbacks from the well, septic, and property lines.

One thing to plan for early is financing. A standard FHA loan will not accept a hauled-water cistern on new construction, since HUD treats cisterns and "other alternative water sources" as not acceptable for mortgage insurance on a new build. That is why many hauled-water homes are built with a well, with cash, or through a local portfolio lender. Hauled water is a reliable, normal way to supply a desert home, but the storage tank, the delivery access, and the loan all need to be planned together before the slab is poured.

Sources

  • EPCOR: Rio Verde Foothills Water Fill Station (start service)
  • HUD: Individual Water Systems (cisterns and FHA financing)

shortDefinition: A header is a horizontal framing beam placed above a door, window, or other opening in a wall. It carries the weight from above and transfers that load around the opening down to the studs on each side, so the opening does not sag or crack the structure.
definitionHtml:
Every door and window interrupts the wall studs that would normally carry the roof and floor loads straight down, so framing needs a beam to pick up that load and reroute it. That beam is the header. It takes the load that would have passed through the missing studs and redirects it around the opening, down through the king and jack studs (also called trimmers) on each side, and into the foundation.

Headers are sized to the span and the load above them. A small interior door might need only a doubled 2x6. A wide great-room window or a multi-car garage door can require a deep engineered beam such as an LVL (laminated veneer lumber) or a steel section. The 2018 International Residential Code, Chapter 6 and Section R602.7, gives the prescriptive header sizes for common spans, and anything beyond those tables gets an engineer's design.

For example, a Scottsdale custom home with a 16-foot multi-slide patio door that opens the great room to the backyard needs a large engineered header above that opening. Skip it or undersize it and the roof load pushes down on an unsupported span, which leads to sagging, sticking doors, and cracked drywall above the opening.

Do not confuse a header with a truss or a beam in general. A truss spans the whole roof; a header spans a single opening in a wall. A header is one specific type of beam, the one that solves the problem of holding the structure up where you cut a hole in the wall for a door or window.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2018 IRC, Chapter 6 Wall Construction
  • International Code Council, 2018 IRC, Section R602.7 (Headers)
metaDescription: A header is the beam above a door or window that carries load around the opening. Here is how headers are sized and used in Arizona home framing.

shortDefinition: A heat pump is a single system that both heats and cools by moving heat instead of burning fuel. In summer it pulls heat out of the house; in winter it pulls heat from outside air into the house. Because it transfers heat rather than generating it, it can deliver two to four times the energy it uses.
definitionHtml:
Run it one direction and it cools like a standard air conditioner, pulling heat out of your home and dumping it outside. Run it the other way and it heats, pulling warmth from the outdoor air and bringing it inside. That reversible trick is what makes a heat pump a single system that both heats and cools by moving heat instead of creating it. The U.S. Department of Energy explains that a heat pump "transfers heat rather than converting it from a fuel, like combustion heating systems." One unit does both jobs.

Because it transfers heat rather than burning anything, a heat pump is very efficient. The DOE notes a properly installed unit "can deliver up to two to four times more heat energy to a home than the electrical energy it consumes," and ENERGY STAR puts the figure at up to three times the electricity used. A heat pump is rated for cooling by SEER2 and for heating by HSPF2, the same scoring family used for high-efficiency AC. An air conditioner cools only; a heat pump is essentially an air conditioner that can also run backward to heat.

For example, Arizona's mild winters are a good fit for a heat pump. A Fountain Hills home rarely sees a hard freeze, so the system spends most of the year cooling and handles winter heating efficiently from outdoor air, often without a gas furnace at all. A mini-split is one common type of heat pump, useful for a casita or an addition where running ductwork is impractical.

A heat pump is not a furnace and not just an AC. It is one system that flips between the two by reversing the direction it moves heat. For a desert home that cools far more than it heats, that combination of summer efficiency and simple winter heating is why heat pumps are common here. We size them to the home with a Manual J load calculation, not by rule of thumb.

Sources

  • U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Saver: Air-Source Heat Pumps
  • ENERGY STAR, Air-Source Heat Pumps
metaDescription: A heat pump heats and cools by moving heat, not burning fuel, delivering 2 to 4 times the energy it uses. Here is how heat pumps fit Arizona desert homes.

shortDefinition: ICF construction builds walls from hollow foam blocks that lock together, get filled with steel and concrete, and stay in place as permanent insulation. The result is a solid, well-insulated wall that suits Arizona's heat by keeping cooling costs and indoor temperature swings low.
definitionHtml:
ICF, short for Insulated Concrete Form, is a wall-building method that uses interlocking hollow blocks made of rigid foam. Crews stack the blocks like large bricks, set steel rebar inside, and fill the hollow core with concrete. The foam is not removed. It stays as a permanent layer of insulation on both faces of the wall, so every ICF wall is concrete in the middle with insulation built in.

The method appeals in Arizona because of the climate. A concrete-cored wall has high thermal mass, meaning it absorbs and releases heat slowly, and the foam adds a continuous insulation layer. Together they smooth out the day-to-night temperature swing and cut the load on the air conditioner through a 110-degree Phoenix summer. The U.S. Department of Energy lists lower energy bills, quieter interiors, and strong, disaster-resistant walls as the main benefits.

For example, a custom home in Cave Creek built with ICF exterior walls can hold a steadier indoor temperature than a comparable wood-frame home, which often shows up as a smaller cooling bill in July and August. The walls also resist fire and high winds better than standard framing.

ICF differs from standard wood-frame construction, where studs and batt insulation form the wall, and from concrete block (CMU), which is masonry without the built-in foam. ICF usually costs more up front than wood framing, so it is an upgrade that trades a higher build price for lower operating cost. On a contract, that choice often shows up as an allowance or a line-item upgrade, and it is worth modeling against expected energy savings before deciding.

Sources

  • U.S. Department of Energy, Building Science Education: Insulated Concrete Forms
  • U.S. Dept. of Housing and Urban Development (HUD USER), Costs and Benefits of Insulating Concrete Forms
metaDescription: ICF (insulated concrete form) construction uses foam forms filled with concrete for strong, insulated walls. Here is how ICF performs in Arizona's heat.

shortDefinition: Impact fees are one-time charges a city or town collects from new construction to help pay for the public infrastructure the growth creates, such as roads, water lines, sewers, and parks. In Arizona they are called development fees, and the amount depends on the jurisdiction and the type of home.
definitionHtml:
The idea behind these charges is simple: growth should help pay for the roads, water and sewer capacity, parks, fire stations, and other infrastructure it creates, rather than shifting that cost to existing residents. That is what impact fees are, one-time charges a city or town adds to a new home to help cover the cost of the public services that new home will use. In Arizona, the legal term is development fees.

The fees are set by the local jurisdiction and usually charged when you pull the building permit, so they are part of your upfront permit costs. Amounts vary widely by city and by the categories that city has adopted, and they can run from a few thousand dollars to well over ten thousand for a single-family home. Fast-growing suburbs on the metro edge often charge more than built-out core cities.

Arizona law tightly controls these fees. A.R.S. 9-463.05 lets a municipality charge development fees only for a list of allowed infrastructure types, requires the fee to be roughly proportionate to the actual burden the new home creates, and bars cities from using the money for unrelated spending. Cities must adopt a written fee schedule and an infrastructure improvements plan to support it.

For example, a custom home in a growing town like Buckeye or Queen Creek may carry development fees covering water, wastewater, roads, parks, and public safety, totaling more than a home of the same size inside the City of Phoenix. Unincorporated county land may have no municipal development fees at all, though other charges still apply.

Impact fees are separate from your plan review and inspection fees, and separate from utility hookup or meter charges, though all of them are collected by the AHJ around permitting. Because the numbers swing so much by location, we price the specific city's current fee schedule into the budget before you commit to a lot.

Sources

  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees; imposition by municipalities)
  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Obtain a Building Permit

shortDefinition: An interest reserve is money built into a construction loan to cover the interest payments during the building phase. Instead of paying out of pocket each month while the home is unfinished, the lender draws the interest from this reserve, so the borrower's cash flow stays clear until the home is done.
definitionHtml:
A construction loan does not hand over the full amount at once. The CFPB explains that the money is released "in a series of advances as the construction progresses," and the borrower owes interest only on the portion drawn so far. An interest reserve is the built-in cushion of money that covers those monthly interest charges, so the borrower is not writing an interest check every month on a home they cannot yet live in.

The mechanics are simple. The lender sets aside a calculated amount inside the loan, and each month it draws that period's interest from the reserve rather than billing you directly. As the build progresses and more of the loan is drawn down on the draw schedule, the interest grows, and the reserve absorbs it. This keeps your cash flow clean during construction, which matters if you are also paying rent or a mortgage on your current home.

The reserve is sized up front from estimates: the loan amount, the expected draw pace, the interest rate, and the construction timeline. Because it is an estimate, two things can go wrong. If construction runs long or rates are variable and climb, the reserve can run dry before the home is finished, and you start paying interest out of pocket. Lenders use methods like those in Regulation Z, Appendix D to estimate and disclose interest on these multiple-advance loans.

For example, an Apache Junction borrower with a 12-month build budgets an interest reserve sized to cover roughly a year of interest on a rising balance. If the build slips to 15 months, the reserve may be exhausted, and the last few months of interest come from the borrower's pocket.

When you compare construction loans, ask whether the interest reserve is funded inside the loan, how it was sized, and what happens if the build runs past schedule. A realistic reserve, tied to an honest timeline, keeps a surprise interest bill from hitting at the worst moment.

Sources

  • U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, What is a construction loan?
  • CFPB, Regulation Z, Appendix D (construction loans; interest)
  • Fannie Mae Selling Guide, Single-Closing Construction-to-Permanent Financing

shortDefinition: The International Fire Code (IFC) is a model code from the International Code Council that sets fire-safety rules for buildings, from sprinklers and fire access to defensible space. Arizona cities adopt and amend it locally. Paradise Valley enforces the 2021 IFC.
definitionHtml:
Fire-sprinkler and fire-alarm requirements, fire-truck access roads and turnarounds, hydrant spacing and water supply, exits, the storage of flammable materials, and defensible space clearing around homes in wildfire-prone foothills are all governed by one model code written by the International Code Council: the International Fire Code (IFC). It covers how buildings and sites are designed and maintained so a fire can be prevented, contained, and fought.

The IFC is a model code, which means it has no legal force until a government adopts it. Arizona has no statewide building code, so each city or county decides which edition to enforce and what local amendments to add. The fire code rides alongside the building code, usually the International Residential Code (IRC) for houses, and the local fire marshal helps enforce it as part of the AHJ.

For example, the Town of Paradise Valley enforces the 2021 IFC. A hillside custom home there can trigger requirements that a flat-lot home in central Phoenix does not, such as a wider fire-access driveway, a residential sprinkler system, and a cleared defensible-space zone around the structure. Those rules are checked during plan review and again at field inspections before the home can be occupied.

The IFC is different from the IRC. The IRC governs how the house itself is built (framing, foundations, energy). The IFC governs fire safety and access on the site and inside the building. Both can apply to the same project. Always confirm the exact edition and amendments your city has adopted, because the year and the local changes decide what you actually have to build.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2021 International Fire Code (IFC)
  • International Code Council, codes.iccsafe.org code library

shortDefinition: The International Residential Code (IRC) is the model building code for one- and two-family homes, written by the International Code Council. Arizona cities and counties adopt it, often the 2018 edition, so it sets the minimum rules your custom home must meet to pass plan review and inspection.
definitionHtml:
The International Code Council (ICC) writes and updates a model code on a three-year cycle, called the International Residential Code, or IRC, that sets the minimum safety and construction standards for detached houses, townhouses, and duplexes up to three stories. The IRC is not law by itself. It becomes law only when a city, county, or state formally adopts it, often with local changes called amendments.

The IRC covers nearly every part of a house: footings and foundations, framing, roofing, energy use, plumbing, mechanical systems, and electrical work. When a builder pulls a permit, the AHJ (the building department) checks the plans and the finished work against the adopted IRC. Pass, and the build moves forward. Fail, and it gets corrected before the next stage.

In the Phoenix metro, most jurisdictions build on the 2018 IRC. Maricopa County, the City of Phoenix, and the City of Scottsdale each adopt the IRC and add local amendments for issues like expansive soil, monsoon drainage, and energy performance in extreme heat. That is why the same code section can be applied a little differently in Cave Creek than in Apache Junction.

For example, the IRC sets the minimum size and depth of a footing, but a Casa Grande lot with clay soil may trigger a stricter, engineer-stamped foundation under the local amendment. The base rule is national; the local layer makes it fit Arizona ground.

The IRC differs from the International Building Code (IBC), which governs commercial and larger multifamily buildings. For a single-family custom home, the IRC is the rulebook, and knowing which edition your city adopted tells you which version of that rulebook your project answers to.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2018 International Residential Code (IRC)
  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Residential Construction
metaDescription: The International Residential Code (IRC) is the model code for houses. Here is how Arizona cities adopt the IRC and what edition applies to your custom home.

shortDefinition: A junior accessory dwelling unit, or JADU, is a small second living space created inside the walls of an existing single-family home, usually up to about 500 square feet. It has its own entrance and a small kitchen, and it may share a bathroom with the main house, unlike a full ADU built as a separate unit.
definitionHtml:
Carving a second home out of an existing house, rather than building a new structure, is the whole idea behind a junior accessory dwelling unit, or JADU. It is built within the walls of the main home, often from a converted bedroom or garage area, and is typically capped around 500 square feet. A JADU has its own exterior entrance and at least a small kitchen, and it may share a bathroom with the primary house.

The point of a JADU is to add a self-contained living space without building a whole new structure. Because it reuses space already inside the house, it usually costs less than a detached unit and avoids new foundation, roofing, and exterior walls. Owners use JADUs for aging parents, adult children, a home office with a kitchenette, or rental income.

The key difference is scale and construction. A full accessory dwelling unit (ADU) is a complete, independent home, often detached, with its own kitchen and bathroom. A casita is the common Arizona term for a guest house or detached ADU. A JADU is smaller, sits inside the existing home, and may share plumbing, so it is the lightest-weight option of the three.

Arizona has moved to make these units easier to add. A.R.S. 9-461.16 directs many municipalities to allow accessory dwelling units on single-family lots and limits how restrictive local rules can be, which has pushed cities across the Phoenix metro to update their codes. Local rules still govern size limits, setbacks, parking, and owner-occupancy, and they vary by city. For example, a homeowner in Tempe or Phoenix might convert part of a large house into a JADU for a parent, while keeping the main home intact. Before designing one, we confirm the specific city's current ADU and JADU rules, since they are changing quickly.

Sources

  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-461.16 (Accessory dwelling units; municipal requirements)
  • U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development, HUD USER (research on accessory dwelling units)

shortDefinition: A lien waiver is a signed document in which a contractor, subcontractor, or supplier gives up the right to file a mechanic's lien for work or materials, usually in exchange for payment. It is the receipt that proves a trade was paid and cannot later claim the home.
definitionHtml:
When a builder, subcontractor, or supplier signs a lien waiver, they release the right to record a mechanic's lien against the property for the work or materials covered by that payment. It is the paperwork that closes the loop on a payment, how an owner turns "I paid the framer" into "the framer has signed away any claim against my house."

Arizona spells out the rules in A.R.S. 33-1008, and it recognizes four standard waiver forms built around two questions. The first question is timing. A conditional waiver only takes effect once the payment actually clears, so it protects the signer if a check bounces. An unconditional waiver releases the lien right immediately, whether or not the money arrived. The second question is scope. A progress waiver covers one draw or one period of work, while a final waiver covers the entire job through completion.

The smart sequence pairs waivers with the draw schedule. As each draw is released, the owner collects a conditional progress waiver from every trade paid in that draw, then confirms the payment cleared. At the end, a final unconditional waiver from each party, including the general contractor, clears the path to a clean title.

For example, before issuing the framing draw on a Scottsdale build, a builder collects a conditional progress lien waiver from the framing crew for that exact dollar amount. When the project finishes, each trade signs a final unconditional waiver. Statute warns that an unconditional waiver is binding even if you were never actually paid, so signers should not hand one over until the funds are in hand.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 33-1008 (Waiver of lien rights; types of waivers)
  • A.R.S. 33-981 (Liens for labor or materials)
metaDescription: A lien waiver is a signed release of lien rights in exchange for payment. Here is how conditional and unconditional lien waivers protect Arizona homeowners.

shortDefinition: Lot coverage is the share of a lot that buildings are allowed to cover, set by zoning as a percentage of the lot area. A 35% maximum on a 10,000-square-foot lot caps the building footprint at 3,500 square feet. It limits how large a home and outbuildings can be on a given parcel.
definitionHtml:
If your zoning sets 35% lot coverage and your lot is 10,000 square feet, the footprint of your home, garage, and covered structures can total no more than 3,500 square feet on the ground. That percentage-of-lot-area math is what lot coverage controls: the zoning rule that caps how much of your lot the buildings can sit on.

The rule controls bulk and density. By limiting footprint, the city keeps open space, drainage area, and breathing room between homes, and it stops a lot from being almost entirely roofed over. What counts toward coverage varies by city: the main house and garage almost always count, while uncovered patios, pools, and driveways often do not, and covered patios or casitas may or may not, depending on the ordinance. Lot coverage works alongside setbacks and height limits, so the buildable footprint is whatever is left after all three rules apply.

For example, a Scottsdale lot might allow a generous footprint by setback alone, but a 35% coverage cap could still hold a single-story home to a smaller floor plate than the owner wanted, pushing the design toward a second story or a more compact footprint.

Lot coverage is different from a setback, which controls distance from the property lines, and from floor area ratio, which some cities use to limit total floor area across all stories. Coverage is strictly about ground footprint. Private CC&Rs can also set a stricter coverage limit than the city. We check the lot-coverage cap early, because it can quietly decide the maximum size of a single-story home before design begins.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale, Zoning Ordinance Resources (district regulations)
  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Residential Construction
metaDescription: Lot coverage is the percentage of a lot that buildings may cover under zoning. Here is how lot coverage limits home size on Arizona lots and how to calculate it.

shortDefinition: Low-E glass is window glass with a microscopically thin metallic coating that reflects heat while letting light through. The coating lowers the window's U-factor and can cut solar heat gain, reducing energy loss by 30% to 50%. In the desert, spectrally selective low-E glass blocks sun heat while keeping daylight.
definitionHtml:
A layer of metal or metal oxide so thin it is invisible to the eye is all that separates Low-E glass from ordinary window glass. "Low-E" stands for low emissivity, meaning the surface gives off very little radiant heat. The coating reflects heat back toward its source while still letting visible light pass, so the window stays clear but moves much less heat. The U.S. Department of Energy reports low-E coatings can reduce energy loss by 30% to 50% compared with plain glass and typically add only about 10% to 15% to the window cost.

The coating works by reflecting infrared energy. In winter it reflects indoor heat back inside; in summer it reflects the sun's heat back outside. A spectrally selective low-E coating goes further, filtering out 40% to 70% of the heat normally transmitted through the glass while still allowing the full amount of daylight. That is the version that matters in Arizona, because it lowers both the window's U-factor and its SHGC without making the home dark.

For example, two new homes in Apache Junction with the same west-facing windows will feel different at 4 p.m. in July. Plain double-pane glass lets the low sun pour heat into the room. Spectrally selective low-E glass reflects most of that heat back outside, so the room stays cooler and the air conditioner cycles less, while the view and daylight look the same.

Low-E glass is the coating, not the whole window. Its real performance shows up in the NFRC-certified U-factor and SHGC numbers on the label, which is what a builder should actually compare. Not all low-E is tuned the same way: a coating optimized for a cold climate captures winter sun, while a desert home wants the spectrally selective type that rejects it. Matching the coating to Arizona's cooling-dominated climate is the whole point.

Sources

  • U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Saver: Window Types and Technologies
  • National Fenestration Rating Council (NFRC), Residential Certification
metaDescription: Low-E glass has a thin coating that reflects heat while passing light. Here is how low-emissivity windows cut solar heat gain on Arizona desert homes.

shortDefinition: Makeup air is fresh outside air brought into a house to replace air pulled out by a strong exhaust fan, usually a large kitchen range hood. The building code requires it once a hood moves more than 400 cubic feet per minute, so the exhaust does not depressurize the tight, sealed home.
definitionHtml:
When a powerful exhaust fan blows air out of a house, that air has to come from somewhere. Makeup air is the controlled supply of outside air brought in to replace it. The most common reason a custom home needs it is a large kitchen range hood. A professional-style hood can pull a lot of air out fast, and without a planned way for air to return, the house fights back.

The building code puts a hard number on it. Under the 2018 International Residential Code, Section M1503.4, any kitchen exhaust hood that can move more than 400 cubic feet per minute (CFM) must be provided with makeup air at roughly the same rate, through a damper that opens automatically when the hood runs. Many high-end ranges come with 600, 900, or 1,200 CFM hoods, so makeup air is a real design item on a luxury kitchen, not an option.

The reason this matters more in a modern home is air tightness. New Arizona custom homes are built tight and sealed (you can confirm tightness with a blower-door test), which is great for energy bills. But a tight house has no easy gaps for replacement air. Run a 900 CFM hood in a sealed house and you create negative pressure that can pull air, and combustion gases, backward down a water-heater or fireplace flue. That backdraft is a carbon-monoxide hazard.

For example, a Paradise Valley kitchen with a 1,200 CFM hood needs a dedicated makeup air unit, often a ducted, damper-controlled, sometimes tempered air supply, sized to match the hood so the house stays balanced when the hood is on high.

Plan makeup air early, with the HVAC and hood selection together. It ties into the home's overall air balance and Manual J load planning, and retrofitting a makeup air path into a finished, tight house is far harder than designing it in from the start.

Sources

  • ICC 2018 International Residential Code, Section M1503.4 (Makeup air required)
  • U.S. DOE / PNNL Building America Solution Center, Kitchen Exhaust
metaDescription: Makeup air replaces air a big range hood pulls out of a home. Here is the IRC 400 CFM rule and why tight Arizona custom homes need a makeup air system.

shortDefinition: A Manual J load calculation is the industry method for sizing a home's heating and cooling system. It measures how much heat the house gains and loses through walls, windows, roof, and air leakage, then sets the exact size of air conditioner or heat pump the home actually needs.
definitionHtml:
How much heating and cooling does a specific house actually need? That question has an industry-standard answer: the Manual J load calculation, published by the Air Conditioning Contractors of America (ACCA) for sizing residential HVAC. Rather than guessing from square footage, Manual J adds up the home's actual heat gain and loss, room by room, using the floor plan, insulation R-values, window type and orientation, ceiling height, air leakage, and the local design temperatures.

The output is a load in BTUs per hour, which tells the builder the correct size of air conditioner, furnace, or heat pump. This matters because both oversizing and undersizing cause real problems. An oversized AC short-cycles, meaning it blasts cold air, hits the thermostat fast, and shuts off before it pulls humidity and evenly cools the house. That leads to hot and cold spots, higher bills, and a unit that wears out early. An undersized unit simply cannot keep up on the worst days.

In Arizona, Manual J carries extra weight because the cooling load is brutal and the design temperature is high. A Phoenix-area Manual J uses a roughly 110 degree outdoor design temperature, so window placement, glass type, and attic insulation swing the result hard. A west-facing wall of glass in Scottsdale can add tons of cooling load that a square-footage rule of thumb would miss entirely.

For example, two 3,000 square foot homes can need very different equipment. One with deep overhangs, low-gain windows, and a tight, well-insulated shell may calculate to 4 tons of cooling. An identical floor plan with big west-facing glass and a leaky envelope can calculate to 5 or 6 tons. Manual J is also the first step before Manual S (equipment selection) and Manual D (duct design). Skipping it and sizing by a flat "one ton per 400 square feet" rule is how homes end up over-cooled, humid, and expensive to run.

Sources

  • Air Conditioning Contractors of America (ACCA), Manual J Residential Load Calculation
  • U.S. DOE Building America Solution Center, Heating/Cooling Equipment Sizing
metaDescription: A Manual J load calculation sizes a home's HVAC to its real heat gain and loss. Here is why Manual J matters for Arizona custom homes in extreme heat.

shortDefinition: A mechanic's lien is a legal claim a contractor, subcontractor, or supplier can record against a property when they have not been paid for labor or materials used to improve it. The recorded lien attaches to the home and can force a sale to collect the unpaid amount.
definitionHtml:
When a contractor, subcontractor, laborer, or material supplier improves a property and does not get paid, Arizona law gives them a way to secure that payment: recording a mechanic's lien (state law calls it a mechanics' and materialmen's lien) against the property in the county where it sits. The lien clouds the title, which means the owner usually cannot sell or refinance cleanly until it is paid or released.

Under A.R.S. 33-981, the right to a lien covers labor, professional services, materials, machinery, and fixtures used to build or improve real property. The claim attaches to the land and the structure, not just to the person who owed the money. That is the part owners miss. You can pay your general contractor in full and still face a lien from a framing sub or a lumber yard the contractor never paid.

For example, a homeowner in Cave Creek hires a builder for a $600,000 custom home. The builder pays most trades but stiffs the electrical sub. That sub can record a mechanic's lien on the finished house for its unpaid balance, even though the owner already paid the builder.

Arizona builds in a warning system. Most subs and suppliers must serve a preliminary twenty day notice under A.R.S. 33-992.01 early in the job. That notice is not a lien. It is a heads-up that the sender has lien rights, and it tells the owner who is on the project. The defenses against a surprise lien are simple in concept: collect a signed lien waiver from each trade as you release each draw, and confirm the notices and waivers match before the final payment goes out.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 33-981 (Liens for labor or materials; lien for assessments)
  • A.R.S. 33-992.01 (Preliminary twenty day notice)
metaDescription: A mechanic's lien is a recorded claim against a home for unpaid construction work. Here is how mechanic's liens work in Arizona and how owners avoid them.

shortDefinition: A mini-split is a ductless heating and cooling system with an outdoor compressor connected to one or more indoor air handlers by a small refrigerant line. Because it has no ductwork, it cools or heats individual rooms directly, avoiding the energy lost through ducts in a hot attic.
definitionHtml:
Skip the ductwork entirely and you get a mini-split: an outdoor unit holding the compressor and condenser, wired to one or more indoor air handlers mounted on a wall, ceiling, or floor inside the home. A slim conduit, about three inches wide, runs between them carrying the refrigerant line, power, and a condensate drain. The indoor unit conditions the air for its room directly, with no ducts in between.

That ductless design is the whole advantage. According to the U.S. Department of Energy, duct losses can account for more than 30 percent of a system's energy use, and the loss is worst when ducts run through an unconditioned attic. In Arizona, where attic temperatures climb well past 130 degrees in summer, ductwork up there bleeds cooling badly. A mini-split delivers its cooling straight to the room and avoids that penalty, and modern units run at high SEER efficiency.

A single outdoor unit can serve several indoor heads in a multi-zone setup, and each head has its own thermostat. That makes mini-splits a strong fit for the spots a central system handles poorly: a casita or guest house, a converted garage, a home office, a sunroom, or an addition where extending the main ducts is impractical.

For example, a detached casita on a Cave Creek property can run on a single mini-split rather than tying into the main home's HVAC, giving the guest space its own independent temperature control and its own efficient zone. The trade-off is that each indoor head is visible on the wall and serves only its area, so whole-house comfort needs a head in every conditioned space. Mini-splits should still be sized with a Manual J load calculation, not by guesswork, for the same reasons any HVAC equipment is.

Sources

  • U.S. Department of Energy, Ductless Mini-Split Heat Pumps
  • U.S. DOE Building America Solution Center, Ductless (Mini-Split) Heat Pumps
metaDescription: A mini-split is a ductless HVAC system that heats and cools rooms without ductwork. Here is where mini-splits make sense on Arizona custom homes and casitas.

shortDefinition: Natural Area Open Space (NAOS) is desert land that must be left in its natural, undisturbed state as a condition of development. In Scottsdale's hillside and Sonoran Desert areas, a set percentage of each lot is recorded as NAOS, where you cannot build, grade, or landscape.
definitionHtml:
Scottsdale's Environmentally Sensitive Lands Ordinance (ESLO) protects the city's Sonoran Desert hillsides, washes, ridgelines, and native vegetation by carving out Natural Area Open Space, abbreviated NAOS: desert that a development must preserve in its natural state forever. When a lot is developed under the ESLO, a portion of it is set aside and recorded as NAOS, and inside that area you cannot build, grade, clear, or install conventional landscaping. It stays raw desert.

NAOS is not a fixed percentage for every lot. Scottsdale calculates the required amount from the land's slope and landform, so steeper, more sensitive terrain must preserve more of the lot. A relatively flat parcel might owe a smaller share, while a hillside lot can owe a large fraction of its area as undisturbed NAOS. The required area is then placed on the recorded plat as an easement-like preservation area, so it binds future owners too.

For a custom home, NAOS directly shrinks your buildable area. It works alongside, but separately from, setbacks and lot coverage limits. A lot can look big on paper, but once you subtract the recorded NAOS, the part you may actually disturb and build on is smaller. That drives where the house, driveway, and pool can go, and it is why desert hillside sites need careful early planning.

For example, a Scottsdale hillside lot in the ESL overlay might be required to keep a substantial percentage of its area as NAOS. The home, driveway, and graded yard all have to fit in the remaining disturbable footprint, with the protected desert left untouched around them.

If you are buying or building on a sensitive Scottsdale lot, confirm the NAOS requirement before you design. Knowing how much of the lot must stay wild, and where, tells you the true size of the canvas you have to work with.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale, Environmentally Sensitive Lands Ordinance (ESLO)
  • City of Scottsdale, Guide to Natural Area Open Space (NAOS)

shortDefinition: An owner-builder is a property owner who builds or improves their own home without holding a contractor's license. Arizona allows this under a narrow exemption, but only if you do the work for your own use and do not offer the structure for sale or rent within one year of completion.
definitionHtml:
Normally Arizona requires a licensed contractor for any building work over $1,000, but A.R.S. 32-1121 carves out an exemption for "owners of property who improve such property or who build or improve structures on such property and who do the work themselves," which is what makes an owner-builder possible: a homeowner acting as their own contractor. In plain terms, you can pull your own permit and build your own house without a contractor's license, as long as you stay inside the rules.

The rules are strict, and the one-year sale limit is the one that trips people up. The exemption only applies if the home is for your own use, not built to sell. A.R.S. 32-1121 says that if you sell, rent, or even offer for sale or rent the structure within one year after completion, that is treated as proof you built it for profit, which means you needed a license and never qualified for the exemption. Build it, move in, keep it: fine. Build it and flip it: not fine.

Being an owner-builder also means you take on the contractor's responsibilities. You pull the permit, schedule and pass every inspection, and you carry the liability if a subcontractor is hurt or does defective work. You are outside the ROC consumer-protection system as the builder, so the Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund does not back your own project, and lenders and insurers often treat an owner-builder loan as higher risk.

For example, a Rio Verde landowner who wants to build on their own lot can legally serve as owner-builder, hire licensed subs for plumbing and electrical, and supervise the job, provided they live in the finished home and do not list it for sale within twelve months.

The owner-builder path saves the general-contractor markup, but it trades that savings for real time, risk, and accountability. It fits a hands-on owner with construction know-how, not someone looking for a shortcut on a complex desert custom home.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 32-1151 (Contracting without a license; prohibition)

shortDefinition: A perc test, short for percolation test, measures how fast water drains through the soil on a lot. It is used to decide whether the ground can absorb wastewater from a septic system. On rural Arizona lots without a sewer hookup, a passing perc test is often required before you can build.
definitionHtml:
A tester digs one or more holes on your lot, fills them with water, and times how fast the level drops. That rate is the percolation rate, and the test that produces it is the perc test, short for percolation test. The number tells an engineer whether the soil can safely absorb the wastewater from a septic system and how large the drain field must be.

The test matters on land that has no connection to a city sewer, which describes many rural and large-lot properties around Rio Verde, Cave Creek, and the desert edges of Pinal County. If you cannot tie into a sewer, you need an onsite wastewater system (a septic system), and the soil has to drain well enough to handle it. Soil that drains too slowly, such as tight expansive clay, or too fast, like loose sand, can fail or force a more expensive engineered system.

For example, a buyer planning a custom home on five acres near Rio Verde would have a perc test done early, often before closing. A passing rate confirms a standard septic system will work and roughly how big the leach field needs to be. A failing rate means a costlier alternative system or, in some cases, that the lot cannot support a home.

In Arizona, onsite wastewater systems are regulated by the Arizona Department of Environmental Quality (ADEQ) and the county's environmental health office, who set how the soil evaluation is done and who must perform it. A perc test is part of the broader site evaluation, which also looks at slope, groundwater, and setbacks from wells. We schedule soil and septic testing before design, because the results decide where the house and drain field can sit on the lot.

Sources

  • U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, Types of Septic Systems
  • Arizona Department of Environmental Quality (onsite wastewater / septic)

shortDefinition: Plan review is the step where a city or county building department checks your home's drawings against the building code and zoning rules before it issues a permit. Reviewers confirm the design is safe and legal, request corrections if needed, and only then approve the project to start construction.
definitionHtml:
Before anyone breaks ground, a building department runs a desk-side check on your construction drawings called plan review. A reviewer reads the plans and confirms they meet the adopted building code, the energy code, and the zoning rules for your lot. If something is missing or wrong, the department issues corrections, and the plans go back for fixes. Once the plans clear, the AHJ issues the building permit.

The 2018 International Residential Code requires submitted construction documents for a permit, and plan review is how the AHJ confirms those documents comply. Reviewers look at the structural design, the foundation, electrical and plumbing layouts, energy details, and whether the house fits inside the setback lines for the lot. On a custom home, the package usually includes architectural plans, an engineered foundation, and a site plan.

For example, a custom home in Scottsdale goes through plan review at the City of Scottsdale, while a build on unincorporated land near Rio Verde is reviewed by Maricopa County. A first submittal often comes back with a round of corrections, such as a missing soil report detail or an energy calculation. The builder resubmits, and the permit issues after the plans pass.

Plan review is different from inspection. Plan review checks the design on paper before construction. Inspection checks the actual work in the field at each stage. Timelines vary by city and workload, often a few weeks for a single-family home, and expedited review is sometimes available for a fee. Getting a clean, complete plan set in on the first try is the fastest way through, so we build the permit package to the local jurisdiction's exact checklist.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2018 IRC, Chapter 1 Scope and Administration (permits and construction documents)
  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Obtain a Building Permit
metaDescription: Plan review is when the building department checks your home plans against the building code before issuing a permit. Here is how plan review works in Arizona.

shortDefinition: A plat is the official recorded map that divides land into lots, showing each lot's boundaries, dimensions, streets, and easements. When a subdivision is recorded, its plat becomes the legal description of every lot in it. Your lot is defined by the plat on file at the county recorder.
definitionHtml:
Your deed can name "Lot 14, Block 2" of a recorded subdivision instead of listing long survey coordinates, and the document that makes that shorthand legal is the plat, the survey map that legally cuts a piece of land into individual lots. It shows every lot's boundary lines and measurements, the streets and alleys, the easements, and the block and lot numbers. Once a plat is approved and recorded at the county recorder's office, it becomes the official legal description for the property.

Arizona counties split the process into two stages. A preliminary plat is the tentative layout the developer submits when dividing land into six or more parcels, reviewed by county or city staff. A final plat is the approved version that gets recorded, which is the one that actually creates the lots and fixes their lines. In Maricopa County, recorded plats live at the recorder's office and are indexed by subdivision name, book, and page.

For example, before buying a lot in a new Rio Verde subdivision, you can pull the recorded plat to see the exact lot dimensions, where the utility and drainage easements run, and how the lot sits against the street. That map controls what you can build, because setbacks and buildable area are measured from the lines it draws.

A plat is the map; the CC&Rs are the separate recorded rules for the subdivision, and an easement is a specific right drawn on the plat. People also confuse a plat with a plot plan, which is the site drawing for one specific house on its lot. The plat defines the lot itself. We start every build by pulling the recorded plat, because its lines and easements decide where the home can legally go.

Sources

  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Plats (subdivision process)
  • Maricopa County Recorder, Map Recordation Criteria
metaDescription: A plat is the recorded map that divides land into lots, showing boundaries, streets, and easements. Here is how plats define Arizona lots and where to find them.

shortDefinition: A post-tension slab is a concrete foundation strengthened by steel cables that are tightened, or tensioned, after the concrete hardens. The cables squeeze the slab together so it resists cracking and movement, which is why it is the standard foundation on Arizona's expansive soils.
definitionHtml:
High-strength steel cables, called tendons, are what set a post-tension slab apart from an ordinary concrete pour: they get pulled tight after the concrete has cured. The crew lays the tendons in a grid before the pour, sheathed in plastic so they can move. Once the concrete reaches strength, usually a few days later, a hydraulic jack stretches each cable and locks it. That squeeze, or compression, holds the slab together as one stiff plate.

The method matters in Arizona because of the ground. Much of Maricopa and Pinal County sits on expansive soil, clay that swells when wet and shrinks when it dries. That movement cracks a plain concrete slab and the home above it. A post-tension slab spreads loads and resists the soil's push and pull far better than a standard slab with rebar alone. The Post-Tensioning Institute notes this is why the design dominates residential foundations across the Southwest.

For example, a custom home in Casa Grande or Queen Creek, where clay soils and land subsidence are common, will almost always sit on a post-tension slab engineered to the specific soil report. A geotechnical engineer tests the lot, and the slab design is stamped before the AHJ approves the permit.

Post-tension differs from a rebar-only slab, where steel bars resist cracking but the concrete is never actively compressed. It also differs from pre-tension, used in factory-made beams where cables are tensioned before the concrete is poured. One practical note for owners: the live cables run through the slab, so you never cut, core, or drill into a post-tension foundation without locating the tendons first.

Sources

  • Post-Tensioning Institute (official organization site)
  • Arizona Geological Survey, Earth Fissures & Subsidence
metaDescription: A post-tension slab uses steel cables tensioned after the concrete cures to resist cracking. Here is why it is the standard on Arizona's expansive soils.

shortDefinition: A punch list is the list of small items a builder must finish or fix before a construction project is considered complete. It is created during a walk-through near the end of the build and covers cosmetic and minor functional defects, like paint touch-ups, sticking doors, or missing hardware.
definitionHtml:
Near the end of construction, the owner and builder walk the house together and note every item that still needs attention: a paint scuff, a sticking cabinet door, a missing outlet cover, a scratch on a counter. That running record of last small tasks is the punch list. Those items go on the list, the builder completes them, and the project closes out.

The punch list is a normal, expected part of every custom home. No build is perfect on the first pass, so the walk-through is the structured way to catch the details. Items are usually cosmetic or minor functional fixes, not major defects. The builder works through the list, and the owner confirms each item is resolved before final payment is released.

For example, on a custom home in Gilbert, a punch-list walk-through might turn up a dozen items: touch up baseboard paint in two rooms, adjust a misaligned shower door, replace a cracked switch plate, and clean paint off a window. The builder fixes them within an agreed window, often a week or two, and the owner signs off.

A punch list is not the same as the final inspection, which is the city or county code check that leads to the certificate of occupancy. The punch list is between you and your builder; the final inspection is with the AHJ. It is also different from a change order, which adds new work for a price. Punch-list items are about completing work already in the contract. In Arizona, the Registrar of Contractors sets workmanship standards a builder must meet, which is part of why a clear, fully closed punch list matters. We treat the punch list as a shared checklist, and we do not call a home done until every item is signed off.

Sources

  • U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, Owning a Home
  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors (workmanship standards and complaints)
metaDescription: A punch list is the final list of small fixes a builder completes before a home is done. Here is how the punch-list walk-through works on Arizona custom homes.

sources.title: U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, Owning a Home

shortDefinition: R-value measures how well insulation resists heat flow. A higher R-value means more resistance and better insulation. It is the standard rating for attic, wall, and roof insulation, and it drives how much you spend cooling an Arizona home through the summer.
definitionHtml:
R-value is the number that tells you how good a piece of insulation is at stopping heat. The "R" stands for resistance: the higher the R-value, the harder it is for heat to pass through. Insulation is rated and sold by R-value, so R-38 attic insulation resists heat far better than R-19, even when both fill the same space.

The rating matters most where you fight heat hardest, which in Arizona is the roof and attic. ENERGY STAR puts the Phoenix metro in its hottest climate zone and recommends an attic insulated to at least R-38, roughly 13 to 14 inches of blown insulation, with R-49 or more giving better results on a custom home. Walls and floors carry their own targets. Most common insulation (fiberglass, cellulose, mineral wool) adds about R-3 to R-3.5 per inch, so reaching a high R-value means real thickness.

For example, two identical homes in Cave Creek, one built to R-30 in the attic and one to R-49, will not cool the same. The R-49 home holds the cool air longer, runs the air conditioner less, and costs less every July. That is why insulation level is a comfort and utility-bill decision, not just a code line.

R-value measures the insulation alone, not the whole assembly. Gaps, compressed batts, and air leaks all cut the real-world performance below the label number, which is why careful installation matters as much as the rating. R-value is also different from a window's U-factor, which rates heat flow the opposite way (lower is better). We spec insulation by R-value for each surface and detail the install so the home actually performs at the number on the label.

Sources

  • ENERGY STAR, Recommended Home Insulation R-Values
  • U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Saver: Insulation
metaDescription: R-value rates how well insulation resists heat flow, with higher numbers meaning better insulation. Here is how R-value works for Arizona homes and summer heat.

shortDefinition: A radiant barrier is a reflective material, usually aluminum foil, installed in an attic to reflect radiant heat from the hot roof instead of absorbing it. It lowers the heat that reaches your ceiling and ductwork, which cuts summer cooling costs in hot, sunny climates like Arizona.
definitionHtml:
Regular insulation slows heat that moves by conduction, but a different path needs a different fix: the radiant heat that pours off the underside of a sun-baked roof deck and beams down onto everything below it. A radiant barrier, a shiny, low-emissivity sheet (most often aluminum foil bonded to kraft paper, plastic film, or roof sheathing), goes in the attic to reflect that heat instead of soaking it up.

In summer the sun heats the roof, and that hot deck radiates heat into the attic. A radiant barrier facing an open air space bounces most of that radiant heat back, so less of it reaches the attic floor, the ceiling, and any ducts running through the attic. The U.S. Department of Energy reports that radiant barriers can cut cooling costs 5% to 10% in a warm, sunny climate. They work best exactly where Arizona homes struggle: hot attics with ductwork in them.

For example, a Scottsdale home with the air handler and ducts in a vented attic loses cooling all July as attic air pushes past 140 degrees. Adding a radiant barrier under the roof rafters drops the radiant load on those ducts, so the air conditioner does not fight a superheated attic as hard. Some homes can even get by with a slightly smaller AC unit.

A radiant barrier is not insulation and has no useful R-value on its own. It does not replace attic insulation; it adds to it. The reflective face has to point at an air gap, and dust on the foil cuts its reflectivity over time, so placement matters. For a tight, efficient roof, many Arizona builders pair a radiant barrier or reflective roof deck with a strong attic R-value rather than choosing one over the other.

Sources

  • U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Saver: Radiant Barriers
  • PNNL Building America Solution Center, Unvented Attic Insulation
metaDescription: A radiant barrier is reflective attic foil that bounces radiant roof heat away. Here is how radiant barriers cut summer cooling bills in Arizona homes.

sources.title: U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Saver: Radiant Barriers

shortDefinition: Retention, also called retainage, is a portion of each construction payment, often 5 to 10 percent, that the owner or lender holds back instead of paying out. The held money is released after the work is finished and accepted, giving the builder an incentive to complete every detail.
definitionHtml:
Retention (the terms retention and retainage mean the same thing) is money the owner or construction lender keeps back from each payment to the builder. Instead of paying 100 percent of a completed milestone, the payer holds a slice, commonly 5 to 10 percent, in reserve. That held money accumulates over the job and is released near the end, once the work is finished and accepted.

The point of retainage is holdback power. It gives the owner a pool of the builder's own earned money that does not get released until the punch list is cleared and the home is fully complete. A builder who has 10 percent of every draw waiting at the finish line has a strong reason to come back and fix the last details rather than walking off to the next project.

Retention plugs directly into the draw schedule. Each time a draw is released, the retainage is subtracted and parked. For example, on a $700,000 Scottsdale build with 10 percent retention, a $140,000 framing draw pays out $126,000, and $14,000 is held. Across the whole project, that hold can total $60,000 or more, released after substantial completion and final acceptance.

Retention is not the same as a deposit or a contingency. A deposit is paid up front; retainage is held back from money already earned. A contingency covers unknown future costs; retainage covers the risk that known work is left unfinished. On a construction loan, the lender often sets the retainage rate and the release conditions, so owners should confirm those terms before signing. We structure clear retention terms so the held amount and its release point are never a surprise at closeout.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors (contracts, payments, and complaints)
  • U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, Construction Loans
metaDescription: Retention (retainage) is money held back from construction draws until the job is done and accepted. Here is how retainage works on Arizona home builds.

shortDefinition: A roof truss is a pre-engineered triangular frame of wood members that supports a roof and ties the structure together. Built in a factory and craned into place, trusses span the home without interior load-bearing walls, carrying the roof load out to the exterior walls.
definitionHtml:
Triangles do not flex out of shape under load the way a rectangle does, and that simple geometry is the whole idea behind a roof truss: a rigid frame, usually made of 2x4 lumber joined into a series of triangles, that holds up the roof. A web of triangles can carry the weight of the roof, the sheathing, the tile, and any snow or wind load, then push that weight outward to the home's exterior walls.

Most trusses are designed by an engineer, built in a factory to exact dimensions, then delivered and lifted onto the walls by crane. Because a truss spans from one outside wall to the other, it usually removes the need for interior load-bearing walls. That is why truss-framed homes can have large open great rooms with no posts in the middle. The 2018 International Residential Code, Chapter 8 and Section R802.10, governs how trusses are designed, braced, and connected.

A truss is different from a rafter and a header. A rafter is a single sloped board, cut on site, that runs from the ridge to the wall (called stick framing). A truss is a complete engineered assembly. A header carries load over an opening like a door or window. A truss carries the whole roof.

For example, a custom home in Rio Verde with a 40-foot-wide great room can be roofed with engineered trusses that span the full width, leaving the interior open with no center wall. In Arizona, truss design accounts for the local wind and, in higher-elevation builds near Cave Creek and the Mogollon Rim corridor, snow load. Cutting or drilling a truss in the field is never allowed without an engineer's sign-off, because it is a calculated assembly and altering one member can compromise the whole frame.

Sources

  • International Code Council, 2018 IRC, Chapter 8 Roof-Ceiling Construction
  • International Code Council, 2018 IRC, Section R802.10 (Wood trusses)
metaDescription: A roof truss is a factory-built triangular frame that supports a roof and spans the home. Here is how trusses work on Arizona custom home roofs.

shortDefinition: An RV garage, also called a motorcoach garage, is an oversized enclosed garage built to store a recreational vehicle indoors. It has a tall door and a high ceiling, usually 14 to 16 feet, to fit a motorhome, plus the depth and utilities a large coach needs.
definitionHtml:
A motorcoach garage keeps a recreational vehicle indoors, out of the driveway and out of a storage lot. Height and depth are what set it apart from a normal garage. A car garage is about 9 to 10 feet tall inside. An RV garage needs a 14 to 16 foot tall door and a 16 to 18 foot ceiling to clear a large motorhome with its rooftop air conditioners. It also runs much deeper, often 40 to 50 feet. That fits a coach up to 45 feet long under the RVIA maximum length standard.

An RV garage is more than a tall box. To service a coach at home, it often adds hookups. Those include a 50-amp, 240-volt RV outlet, a water line, a sewer dump or macerator, and ventilation for engine and battery fumes. In Arizona, owners add a mini-split for cooling. The slab is usually thicker than a car-garage slab to carry a loaded motorhome, which can weigh more than 30,000 pounds.

For example, a custom home in Rio Verde might include a detached 16-foot-tall, 45-foot-deep RV garage with a 14-foot door, a 50-amp pedestal, and a dump connection, placed to clear the lot's setbacks. Because it is usually a tall accessory building, an RV garage is governed by local height and setback rules and by lot-coverage limits. In unincorporated Maricopa County, a detached accessory building may reach 30 feet in height and may not occupy more than 30% of a required yard. Some owners add a casita above or beside the bay for living space, which makes the structure both a garage and an accessory dwelling.

Sources

  • RV Industry Association (RVIA): RV Dimensions policy (length and width standards)
  • ICC IRC 2018 Section R309 (Garages and Carports)
  • Maricopa County: Accessory Structures, Equipment, and Setbacks (height and setback rules)

shortDefinition: SEER (Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio) rates how efficiently an air conditioner or heat pump cools over a season. A higher SEER means lower cooling bills. SEER2 is the updated federal test that replaced SEER on January 1, 2023, with slightly lower numbers for the same units.
definitionHtml:
SEER stands for Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio, the score that tells you how much cooling an air conditioner or heat pump delivers for the electricity it uses across a cooling season. It is the cooling work done divided by the energy consumed, so a higher SEER unit gives you the same cool air for fewer watts and a smaller summer bill.

On January 1, 2023, the U.S. Department of Energy switched the test procedure and the label to SEER2, which measures the same thing under more realistic conditions (higher static pressure, closer to a real duct system). Because the test is tougher, a unit's SEER2 number is a little lower than its old SEER number for the same hardware. A model rated 15 SEER is about 14.3 SEER2. The federal minimum for new split-system air conditioners in the South, which includes Arizona, is 14.3 SEER2.

For example, in the Phoenix metro, where the air conditioner runs from spring into fall, the gap between a 14.3 SEER2 builder-grade unit and an 18 SEER2 unit shows up on every summer power bill. The higher-efficiency system costs more up front and pays it back over years of long cooling seasons. Heat pumps carry a matching heating score, HSPF2, that replaced HSPF on the same date.

SEER2 rates seasonal performance, while EER2 rates efficiency at one hot design point, useful in a desert climate where peak load matters. SEER2 is not a quality grade by itself. Right-sizing the unit, sealing the ducts, and the insulation R-value all decide whether you get the efficiency you paid for. We size and spec HVAC to the home and the Arizona load, not to a sticker number alone.

Sources

  • U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Saver: Central Air Conditioning
  • ENERGY STAR, Central Air Conditioners (efficiency criteria)
metaDescription: SEER and SEER2 rate cooling efficiency, with higher numbers meaning lower bills. Here is how SEER2 (the 2023 federal standard) affects Arizona HVAC choices.

shortDefinition: A semi-custom home is built from a builder's existing floor plan that you then personalize, choosing finishes and select structural options instead of designing from scratch. It sits between a production home, where choices are limited, and a fully custom home, where every detail is drawn for your lot and needs.
definitionHtml:
Starting from a floor plan the builder already has, then making it yours, is the whole model behind a semi-custom home. You pick finishes (flooring, cabinets, countertops, fixtures) and usually choose from a set menu of structural options, like adding a third bay to the garage, extending a patio, or swapping a den for a fourth bedroom. You are not designing a one-of-a-kind house, but you are far from stuck with a fixed package.

The model exists to balance cost, speed, and choice. Starting from a proven plan saves the time and expense of full architectural design and engineering, and the builder already knows how that plan performs and prices out. You still get real personalization, especially in finishes and a few layout changes, without the longer timeline and higher cost of a fully custom build.

Semi-custom sits in the middle of three common paths. A production home (or spec home) is built to a set plan with limited, pre-chosen options, often on a lot the builder owns. A fully custom home is designed from scratch for your specific lot, lifestyle, and budget, with no fixed starting plan. A semi-custom home borrows the proven plan from production building and the choice of custom building.

For example, a family building in a Gilbert or Queen Creek community might select a semi-custom plan, then choose upgraded tile, a larger kitchen island, and a covered outdoor living space, with much of that handled through allowances in the contract. We offer semi-custom builds for owners who want meaningful choice and a faster, more predictable path than a ground-up custom design, and we are clear from the start about which options are fixed and which are open.

Sources

  • National Association of Home Builders (home building types and process)
  • U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, Owning a Home
metaDescription: A semi-custom home starts from an existing floor plan you personalize. Here is how semi-custom compares to production and fully custom homes in Arizona.

shortDefinition: A septic system is an on-site way to treat household wastewater where there is no city sewer. Waste flows to a buried septic tank that separates solids, then the liquid drains into a leach field where soil filters it. Most rural Arizona custom homes rely on one.
definitionHtml:
Where a city sewer line does not reach, a home has to treat its own wastewater underground instead, and that is exactly what a septic system does: it works as a self-contained sewer for a single property. The U.S. EPA describes the basic design: all the drains in the house flow to a buried, watertight septic tank, where solids settle to the bottom as sludge and grease floats to the top as scum. The clarified liquid in the middle flows out to a leach field (also called a drainfield), where it slowly soaks into the soil and natural filtering finishes the job.

In the Phoenix metro, septic is the norm for rural custom homes: Rio Verde Foothills, the desert north of Cave Creek, much of unincorporated Maricopa County, and parts of Pinal County around Apache Junction and Casa Grande. If your lot has no sewer at the street, you build a septic system, and that is a permitted, engineered part of the home, not an afterthought.

Two Arizona rules drive the design. First, you usually need a percolation test (perc test) or soil evaluation to prove the ground absorbs water well enough for a leach field. Slow-draining or expansive soil can force a larger field or an engineered "alternative" system, which costs more. Second, septic installs are permitted through the county environmental health office (Maricopa County Environmental Services or Pinal County), with required inspections before backfill.

For example, a Rio Verde owner on a five-acre parcel might budget $15,000 to $35,000 or more for a conventional tank-and-leach-field system, with the figure climbing if the perc test demands an engineered design or a larger field.

Septic also shapes other site choices. State and county rules set minimum separation distances between the system and any well, property lines, and washes, so your septic location, well, and home footprint all get planned together early, before you set the foundation.

Sources

  • U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, How Your Septic System Works
  • U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, Types of Septic Systems
  • Arizona Department of Environmental Quality (on-site wastewater)

shortDefinition: A setback is the minimum distance your home must sit from a property line, street, or other feature, set by local zoning. In Arizona, front setbacks often run 20 to 25 feet and side setbacks 5 to 10 feet, but the exact numbers depend on your city and zone.
definitionHtml:
Zoning law requires open space between your building and the edges of your lot, called a setback. It sets a minimum distance from each property line, and nothing permanent (no house, garage, or casita) may cross that line. Setbacks keep homes from crowding the street and each other, protect light and air, and leave room for utilities and emergency access.

Every lot has front, side, and rear setbacks, and the numbers come from your zoning district, enforced by the AHJ. Typical single-family zoning in the Phoenix metro runs a front setback around 20 to 25 feet, side setbacks of 5 to 10 feet, and a rear setback of 15 to 25 feet. Larger desert lots in Rio Verde or Cave Creek often carry deeper setbacks, while older Phoenix neighborhoods may allow less. Corner lots usually have two "front" setbacks, one for each street.

For example, if your Scottsdale lot requires a 25-foot front setback and you want a deep front porch, the porch and its roof must stay behind that line, or you apply for a variance. Eaves, patio covers, and air-conditioning units have their own allowances and sometimes may project a short distance into a setback.

Setbacks shape what you can build before design even starts. They set the buildable area, the footprint left after you subtract the setbacks from the lot. A wide lot with deep setbacks can leave a surprisingly narrow building pad. We check setbacks and the buildable area on day one, because they decide whether your floor plan fits the lot or needs to change.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale, Zoning Ordinance Resources (district regulations and setbacks)
  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Residential Construction

shortDefinition: A soil report, also called a geotechnical report, is an engineer's study of a lot's soil that tells the foundation designer how strong the ground is and how it moves. It comes from drilled or dug test borings and drives the foundation design. Most Arizona custom home permits require one.
definitionHtml:
A crew drills or digs borings across the lot and pulls soil samples, then a lab measures how strong the soil is, how much it swells or shrinks with water, how it drains, and how deep firm material lies. Those findings, written up by a licensed engineer with specific foundation recommendations, make up the soil report, also called a geotechnical report.

The report matters because the foundation is only as good as the assumptions under it. In central Arizona, the big questions are expansive clay, collapsible soils, caliche layers, and land subsidence. Each one changes how a slab must be built. The report gives the structural engineer the soil's bearing capacity and swell potential. Those numbers set the footing sizes, the slab type, and any need for over-excavation, imported fill, or moisture control. The International Residential Code lets building officials require this study, and on questionable soils the Arizona permit will not move without it.

For example, two lots in Queen Creek a half-mile apart can return very different reports, one calling for a standard post-tension slab and the other for deeper grade beams and piers because the clay runs deeper. Designing both the same way would risk cracks on the worse lot.

A soil report is not a perc test, which measures how fast water drains for a septic system, though both involve digging into the lot. The soil report is about supporting the structure. We order the geotechnical report early, before the foundation is designed, because it is the single document that decides whether the slab will hold up on Arizona ground.

Sources

  • Arizona Geological Survey
  • International Code Council, 2021 IRC Chapter 4 (Foundations)
metaDescription: A soil report (geotechnical report) studies a lot's soil to guide foundation design. Here is what it covers and why Arizona custom home permits require one.
